concepts.label: Activation Function
concepts.def_snippet: The nonlinear function applied to a neuron's weighted sum, which gives a neural network the ability to model nonlinear relationships; without it, stacked layers would collapse into a single linear map.

concepts.label: Activation Patching
concepts.def_snippet: A causal localization technique that copies an internal activation from one forward pass into another and measures how the output changes, isolating which components actually carry a given piece of information rather than merely correlating with it.

concepts.label: Activation Steering
concepts.def_snippet: A control technique that edits a model's behavior at inference time by adding a direction vector into its hidden activations, shifting the representation toward or away from a target concept without changing any weights.

concepts.label: Active Learning
concepts.def_snippet: A setup where the model chooses which examples it most wants labeled, querying a human for the most informative points instead of a random sample, so a strong model can be trained with far fewer labels. Its hidden danger is that the model picks its own training data using its current, imperfect beliefs, and so can systematically avoid what it does not know it is missing.

concepts.label: Actor-Critic
concepts.def_snippet: A reinforcement learning architecture that combines a policy (the actor) with a learned value estimator (the critic), using the critic's estimates to reduce the variance of the policy's gradient updates.

concepts.label: AdaGrad
concepts.def_snippet: An optimizer that gives each parameter its own learning rate by dividing the global rate by the square root of the sum of that parameter's past squared gradients, so rarely updated parameters take large steps and frequently updated ones take small steps.

concepts.label: Adam
concepts.def_snippet: The default optimizer of deep learning, combining momentum with a per-parameter learning rate by tracking running averages of both the gradient and its square. Dividing by the second of these nearly cancels each gradient's scale, so every parameter takes a similarly sized step, which is why Adam works with almost no tuning.

concepts.label: Advantage Function
concepts.def_snippet: The quantity that measures how much better taking a particular action in a state is than the state's average value, computed as the action value minus the state value; it is the low-variance learning signal at the heart of modern policy optimization.

concepts.label: adversarial AI
concepts.def_snippet: Adversarial AI refers to AI systems designed or manipulated to deceive, attack, or circumvent other AI systems or human oversight through intentionally deceptive behavior.

concepts.label: agent design
concepts.def_snippet: An AI agent design is a system architecture that equips language models with tools, memory, and decision-making loops to autonomously accomplish multi-step tasks.

concepts.label: Agent Evaluation
concepts.def_snippet: The practice of measuring how well an agent performs multi-step, tool-using tasks, judged on whether it reached the goal and how it got there, which is much harder than scoring a single model output because success unfolds across a trajectory of actions.

concepts.label: Agent Memory
concepts.def_snippet: The mechanisms an agent uses to retain and recall information across the steps of a task and across separate sessions, compensating for the fact that the underlying language model is stateless between calls and limited by its context window.

concepts.label: agent optimization
concepts.def_snippet: Agent optimization refers to the process of tailoring AI agent capabilities and behaviors to match specific model architectures, tasks, or temporal constraints rather than using un…

concepts.label: Agent Orchestration
concepts.def_snippet: The coordination layer that directs how one or more agents and their tasks are sequenced, routed, and combined, controlling the flow of work between steps and sub-agents rather than the reasoning inside any single step.

concepts.label: agent wrapper
concepts.def_snippet: An agent wrapper is a software framework that manages the interface between an AI agent and external tools or systems, handling input/output formatting and execution logic.

concepts.label: agentic AI
concepts.def_snippet: Agentic AI refers to AI systems that autonomously perceive their environment, make decisions, and take actions toward goals rather than simply responding to prompts.

concepts.label: Agentic RAG
concepts.def_snippet: Retrieval-augmented generation driven by an agent that decides when and what to retrieve, issues multiple queries, evaluates the results, and iterates, rather than a single fixed retrieve-then-generate step, so RAG can handle multi-hop and open-ended questions.

concepts.label: Agentic Workflow
concepts.def_snippet: A task structured so that one or more language models drive it through multiple steps of reasoning, tool use, and self-correction, sitting between a single fixed prompt and a fully autonomous agent in how much latitude the model is given.

concepts.label: agentic workflows
concepts.def_snippet: Agentic workflows are systems where AI agents autonomously execute multi-step tasks by perceiving conditions, making decisions, and taking actions toward defined goals.

concepts.label: AGI
concepts.def_snippet: Artificial General Intelligence is a hypothetical AI system that can understand and perform any intellectual task that humans can, though experts disagree on whether it is near, de…

concepts.label: AI agency strategy
concepts.def_snippet: AI agency strategy refers to business approaches for building and scaling consulting or services firms that implement AI solutions for clients.

concepts.label: AI Agent
concepts.def_snippet: A system that wraps a language model in a loop where the model decides actions, calls tools, observes the results, and repeats until a goal is met, rather than producing a single answer in one pass.

concepts.label: AI agents
concepts.def_snippet: AI agents are software systems that can perceive their environment, make decisions, and take actions toward goals with minimal human intervention.

concepts.label: AI Alignment
concepts.def_snippet: The problem of building AI systems whose goals and behavior reliably match the intentions and values of their designers and users, especially as the systems grow more capable.

concepts.label: AI Operating Systems
concepts.def_snippet: The idea that AI systems can manage and automate multiple tasks independently, raising questions about whether specialized AI platforms will become essential infrastructure that pe…

concepts.label: AI regulation
concepts.def_snippet: AI regulation refers to government rules and legal frameworks that govern the development, deployment, and use of artificial intelligence systems to address safety, privacy, bias,…

concepts.label: AI risk
concepts.def_snippet: AI risk refers to the concern that advanced AI systems could cause harm through unintended consequences, misuse, or loss of human control.

concepts.label: AI Safety
concepts.def_snippet: The field concerned with building and deploying AI systems that do not cause harm, spanning the alignment of system goals, robustness to failure and attack, monitoring, and the governance of powerful systems.

concepts.label: AI Winter
concepts.def_snippet: A collapse of funding and faith in AI, triggered when grand promises outrun what the technology can deliver. The field has frozen over at least twice, and the pattern, hype, disappointment, retreat, then a thaw driven by a genuinely new idea, is the single most useful thing to know when judging any AI claim, including today's.

concepts.label: AI-assisted coding
concepts.def_snippet: AI-assisted coding is a technique where language models suggest or generate code snippets, functions, or entire programs based on developer input, reducing manual writing and accel…

concepts.label: Approximate Nearest Neighbor
concepts.def_snippet: A family of algorithms that find vectors close to a query vector without checking every candidate, trading a small, tunable loss in accuracy for an enormous gain in speed, which is what makes vector search practical at scale.

concepts.label: Artificial General Intelligence
concepts.def_snippet: A hypothetical AI system with the broad, flexible, general-purpose cognitive ability of a human being, able to learn and reason across arbitrary domains rather than excelling at narrow tasks, and a focal point for debates about the nature and limits of machine mind.

concepts.label: Artificial Intelligence
concepts.def_snippet: The field of building machines that do things normally taken to require human intelligence: perception, reasoning, language, planning. Its boundary keeps moving, because each problem once solved tends to be downgraded to ordinary computation, so the name marks a receding frontier as much as a fixed set of techniques.

concepts.label: async agents
concepts.def_snippet: Async agents are AI systems that execute tasks independently over time without requiring continuous user interaction, useful for automating multi-step workflows that don't need rea…

concepts.label: Attention Mechanism
concepts.def_snippet: A mechanism that lets a model compute each output as a weighted sum over all input positions, with the weights determined by learned relevance, so it can draw on the most pertinent context regardless of distance.

concepts.label: Attention Schema Theory
concepts.def_snippet: A theory of consciousness proposing that the brain constructs a simplified internal model of its own attention, an attention schema, and that subjective awareness is what that model reports; awareness is the brain's coarse self-description of the act of attending.

concepts.label: Attention Sink
concepts.def_snippet: The empirical tendency of transformers to dump a large share of attention weight onto a few early tokens, often the very first one, which act as a no-op drain that the softmax needs, with consequences for streaming and long-context inference.

concepts.label: Attribution Graph
concepts.def_snippet: A graph that traces how interpretable features causally influence one another to produce a model's output on a specific input, used to reverse-engineer the multi-step reasoning behind a single response and turn it into a readable circuit.

concepts.label: Autoencoder
concepts.def_snippet: A neural network trained to reconstruct its own input through a narrow bottleneck, forcing it to learn a compressed latent representation that captures the data's most salient structure.

concepts.label: Automatic Differentiation
concepts.def_snippet: A family of techniques that compute exact derivatives of a function expressed as a program by applying the chain rule mechanically over its elementary operations, the engine that makes training large neural networks by gradient descent practical.

concepts.label: Automatic Speech Recognition
concepts.def_snippet: The task of transcribing spoken audio into text, a core modality bridge for AI; modern systems are large neural models, often encoder-decoder transformers like Whisper, trained on vast audio-text pairs and robust across accents, noise, and languages.

concepts.label: automation
concepts.def_snippet: Automation uses software or AI systems to perform repetitive tasks or workflows without human intervention, increasing efficiency and reducing manual effort.

concepts.label: Autonomous Agent
concepts.def_snippet: An agent given a high-level goal and broad freedom to decide its own steps, choosing what to do and which tools to use over a long horizon with little or no human intervention along the way.

concepts.label: Autoregressive Model
concepts.def_snippet: A generative model that factorizes a joint distribution into a product of conditionals and generates data one element at a time, each new element conditioned on those already produced.

concepts.label: autoresearch
concepts.def_snippet: Autoresearch is a tool that automatically identifies knowledge gaps in Claude's responses and conducts research to fill them without manual intervention.

concepts.label: Backpropagation
concepts.def_snippet: The algorithm that computes the gradient of a neural network's loss with respect to every weight by applying the chain rule backward through the network, making gradient-based training of deep models efficient and practical.

concepts.label: Bag of Words
concepts.def_snippet: A foundational text representation that describes a document by which words it contains and how often, discarding word order entirely, turning text into a fixed-length vector of term counts over a vocabulary.

concepts.label: Basis
concepts.def_snippet: A minimal set of independent directions from which every vector in a space can be built in exactly one way, the coordinate system that turns abstract vectors into concrete number lists. Choosing the right basis is often the whole difference between a problem that looks tangled and the same problem made simple.

concepts.label: Batch Normalization
concepts.def_snippet: A training technique that normalizes each layer's pre-activations across the current mini-batch to zero mean and unit variance, then rescales them with learned parameters, which stabilizes and accelerates the training of deep networks.

concepts.label: Batch Size
concepts.def_snippet: The number of training examples used to compute each gradient update; it trades off the stability of the gradient estimate, memory use, and training speed, and interacts closely with the learning rate.

concepts.label: Bayes' Theorem
concepts.def_snippet: A rule for inverting conditional probabilities, computing the probability of a hypothesis given evidence from the probability of the evidence given the hypothesis, the prior on the hypothesis, and the overall probability of the evidence.

concepts.label: Bayesian Brain
concepts.def_snippet: The hypothesis that the brain represents the world with probability distributions and updates them by approximate Bayesian inference, combining prior expectations with incoming sensory evidence to infer the hidden causes of its sensations.

concepts.label: Bayesian Epistemology
concepts.def_snippet: The theory that rational belief comes in degrees obeying the probability axioms and that learning from evidence is updating those degrees by conditionalization, supplying a normative account of inference that underpins both philosophy of science and machine learning.

concepts.label: Bayesian Inference
concepts.def_snippet: A framework that treats unknown quantities as random variables with probability distributions, starting from a prior and using observed data to compute a posterior distribution of belief through Bayes' theorem.

concepts.label: Bayesian Optimization
concepts.def_snippet: A sample-efficient global optimization method for expensive black-box functions that fits a probabilistic surrogate model of the objective and uses it to decide, through an acquisition function, the single most informative point to evaluate next.

concepts.label: Beam Search
concepts.def_snippet: A decoding strategy that keeps the several most probable partial sequences at every step, expanding all of them and pruning back to a fixed beam width, so it searches for a high-probability whole sequence rather than the best token at each step.

concepts.label: Behavior Cloning
concepts.def_snippet: The simplest form of imitation learning, treating each expert state-action pair as a supervised example and training a policy to predict the expert's action, learning to act with no reward but suffering from compounding errors off the demonstrated path.

concepts.label: Bellman Equation
concepts.def_snippet: A recursive consistency condition stating that the value of a state equals the immediate reward plus the discounted value of the states that follow, providing the mathematical foundation for nearly every reinforcement learning algorithm.

concepts.label: benchmark
concepts.def_snippet: A benchmark is a standardized test or dataset that measures and compares the performance of AI systems on specific tasks or capabilities.

concepts.label: Benchmark Contamination
concepts.def_snippet: The leakage of benchmark test data into a model's training corpus, so that high scores reflect memorization of the answers rather than genuine capability. It silently inflates reported results and undermines comparison.

concepts.label: BERT
concepts.def_snippet: A transformer-based language model from Google, released in 2018, that learns deeply bidirectional contextual word representations by pretraining on masked-language-modeling, and is fine-tuned for a wide range of NLP tasks.

concepts.label: BERTScore
concepts.def_snippet: A text-generation metric that scores a candidate against a reference by matching their contextual word embeddings rather than exact words, so a correct paraphrase with different vocabulary is credited where surface-overlap metrics like BLEU and ROUGE penalize it.

concepts.label: Best-of-N Sampling
concepts.def_snippet: An inference strategy that generates N candidate responses and selects the best according to a scorer (a reward model, a verifier, or a heuristic), spending parallel compute to lift quality on tasks where judging an answer is easier than producing one.

concepts.label: Bi-Encoder
concepts.def_snippet: A retrieval architecture that encodes a query and a document independently into separate vectors and scores their relevance by a simple similarity between those vectors, which makes document embeddings precomputable and indexable, enabling fast search over a large corpus.

concepts.label: Bias-Variance Tradeoff
concepts.def_snippet: The classical decomposition of a model's error into bias, the error from being too simple, and variance, the error from being too sensitive to the training sample, which pull against each other so neither reaches zero alone. It is the organizing principle of classical machine learning, and the place modern deep learning most visibly broke the rules.

concepts.label: BLEU Score
concepts.def_snippet: An n-gram overlap metric for machine translation that scores a candidate against one or more reference translations by modified n-gram precision, multiplied by a brevity penalty. Higher is better, on a 0 to 1 scale.

concepts.label: BM25
concepts.def_snippet: A classic lexical ranking function that scores how well a document matches a query from the overlapping terms, weighting each term by its rarity and saturating the contribution of repeated occurrences while correcting for document length, and remaining a strong baseline and the standard sparse component in hybrid search.

concepts.label: Byte-Pair Encoding
concepts.def_snippet: A subword tokenization algorithm that builds a vocabulary by repeatedly merging the most frequent adjacent pair of symbols in a corpus, balancing a compact vocabulary against the ability to represent any input.

concepts.label: Calibration
concepts.def_snippet: The property that a model's stated confidence matches its actual accuracy. A well-calibrated model that says it is 80 percent sure is right about 80 percent of the time. Measured by expected calibration error and visualized with reliability diagrams.

concepts.label: Catastrophic Forgetting
concepts.def_snippet: The tendency of a neural network to lose previously learned knowledge when trained on new data, because the same weights that stored the old skill are overwritten while learning the new one.

concepts.label: Category Theory
concepts.def_snippet: A highly general branch of mathematics that studies structures through the structure-preserving maps between them rather than their internal contents, organizing objects, arrows, and their compositions into categories.

concepts.label: Causal Attention
concepts.def_snippet: Self-attention with a mask that stops each position from attending to future tokens, enforcing the left-to-right constraint that lets a model train on next-token prediction over a whole sequence at once; the mechanism that makes decoder-only LLMs autoregressive.

concepts.label: Cell Assembly
concepts.def_snippet: A group of neurons wired together by repeated co-activation into a functional unit, proposed by Donald Hebb as the brain's basic representation: a concept or percept is encoded by the coordinated firing of an entire assembly rather than by any single cell.

concepts.label: Central Limit Theorem
concepts.def_snippet: The result that the sum or average of many independent random variables tends toward a Gaussian distribution regardless of the original distribution, explaining why the normal distribution appears so pervasively in statistics and machine learning.

concepts.label: Chain of Thought
concepts.def_snippet: A prompting and generation technique in which a model is induced to produce intermediate reasoning steps before its final answer, which improves accuracy on multi-step problems by letting the model work through them in its output.

concepts.label: Chain Rule
concepts.def_snippet: The rule for differentiating composed functions: the derivative of a composition is the product of the derivatives of its parts, so influence multiplies along the chain. It is the mathematical foundation of backpropagation, and the same multiplication that makes it powerful is what makes deep networks fragile.

concepts.label: Chatbot Arena
concepts.def_snippet: A live evaluation platform where anonymous users compare two models' responses to the same prompt side by side and vote for the better one, aggregating the head-to-head votes into an Elo-style leaderboard that reflects real human preference rather than a fixed benchmark.

concepts.label: Chinese Room
concepts.def_snippet: A thought experiment by John Searle (1980) arguing that running the right program is not sufficient for genuine understanding, because a person manipulating symbols by rule can produce fluent answers in a language they do not understand at all.

concepts.label: Chunking
concepts.def_snippet: The step in a retrieval pipeline that splits long documents into smaller passages before embedding, so each stored vector represents a focused unit of text that can be retrieved and cited on its own.

concepts.label: Circuit
concepts.def_snippet: A subgraph of features and the weighted connections between them that together implement a specific, human-understandable computation inside a neural network, and the basic unit of explanation in mechanistic interpretability.

concepts.label: Classification
concepts.def_snippet: The supervised task of sorting an input into one of a fixed set of categories, the default shape of a machine learning problem. Its deepest reach is hidden in plain sight: a language model predicting its next token is doing classification over the vocabulary, so the task that recognizes spam also writes prose.

concepts.label: Classifier-Free Guidance
concepts.def_snippet: A sampling technique for conditional diffusion models that strengthens adherence to a condition, such as a text prompt, by extrapolating away from the model's unconditional prediction, with no separate classifier required.

concepts.label: Claude Code
concepts.def_snippet: Claude Code is a feature within Claude that lets users write, execute, and iterate on code directly in conversations for tasks like data analysis, automation, and application devel…

concepts.label: CLIP
concepts.def_snippet: A model trained to align images and text in a shared embedding space by contrastive learning over large numbers of image-caption pairs, enabling zero-shot classification and image-text retrieval.

concepts.label: Clustering
concepts.def_snippet: The unsupervised task of grouping data so that items within a group are more alike than items across groups, with no labels to say what the groups should be. Its defining difficulty is that the task is ill-posed: with no ground truth, there is no single right answer, and the notion of "similar" you choose quietly determines what you find.

concepts.label: code generation
concepts.def_snippet: Code generation is a tool that automatically writes or completes computer code based on natural language descriptions, code context, or examples provided by users.

concepts.label: Code Interpreter
concepts.def_snippet: A tool that lets a model write code, run it in a sandboxed environment, and read back the output, turning a probabilistic text generator into a system that can compute exact answers, manipulate data and files, and verify its own work by execution.

concepts.label: Collective Communication
concepts.def_snippet: The set of group communication primitives, such as all-reduce, all-gather, and reduce-scatter, that synchronize data across many accelerators in distributed training, and whose efficiency over the device interconnect determines how well training scales to large clusters.

concepts.label: Computational Theory of Mind
concepts.def_snippet: The theory that the mind is a computational system and that thinking is, at bottom, the rule-governed manipulation of mental representations, providing the foundational rationale for the project of artificial intelligence.

concepts.label: compute efficiency
concepts.def_snippet: The question of whether AI progress should prioritize maximizing capabilities or minimizing the computational resources required to achieve them.

concepts.label: Computer Use
concepts.def_snippet: An agent capability in which a model operates a computer the way a person does, by viewing the screen and issuing mouse and keyboard actions, letting it use arbitrary software through its graphical interface rather than a bespoke API.

concepts.label: Concentration Inequality
concepts.def_snippet: A mathematical bound showing that the average of many independent random quantities is very unlikely to fall far from its expected value. Concentration inequalities are the engine beneath every generalization guarantee in learning theory.

concepts.label: Conditional Probability
concepts.def_snippet: The probability of one event given that another event is known to have occurred, defined as the joint probability of both divided by the probability of the conditioning event.

concepts.label: Conditional Random Field
concepts.def_snippet: A probabilistic model for sequence labeling that predicts an entire label sequence jointly, modeling the dependencies between adjacent labels, which made it the dominant pre-neural method for tasks like named-entity recognition and part-of-speech tagging.

concepts.label: Confusion Matrix
concepts.def_snippet: A table that breaks a classifier's predictions down by predicted class against true class, turning a single accuracy number into a full picture of which classes it confuses with which. Its real value is that it separates the two kinds of error a single number fuses together, false alarms and misses, which usually carry very different costs.

concepts.label: Conjugate Gradient
concepts.def_snippet: An iterative optimization method that minimizes a quadratic function by stepping along a sequence of mutually conjugate directions, achieving fast convergence using only gradients and no explicit curvature matrix.

concepts.label: Connectionism
concepts.def_snippet: The rival school to symbolic AI, which holds that intelligence is not stored in rules but emerges from the connections among many simple units, learned from data as adjustable weights. It is the intellectual home of the neural network, and over the long run it won.

concepts.label: Consistency Model
concepts.def_snippet: A generative model trained so that every point along a diffusion trajectory maps to the same clean origin, letting it produce a high-quality sample in one or a few steps instead of the dozens or hundreds a diffusion model needs.

concepts.label: Constitutional AI
concepts.def_snippet: An alignment method that trains a model to be helpful and harmless using AI-generated feedback guided by a written set of principles (a constitution), reducing reliance on human-labeled comparisons.

concepts.label: context management
concepts.def_snippet: Context management in AI systems refers to techniques for selecting, organizing, and updating the information provided to models to maintain relevance and prevent degradation from…

concepts.label: context stacking
concepts.def_snippet: Context stacking is a technique that organizes and layers multiple pieces of information or documents to help language models process complex tasks by maintaining relevant informat…

concepts.label: Context Window
concepts.def_snippet: The maximum number of tokens a language model can attend to at once, bounding how much text it can read and generate in a single pass and acting as its working memory.

concepts.label: Continuous Batching
concepts.def_snippet: An LLM serving technique that adds and evicts requests from the running batch at every decoding step rather than waiting for a whole batch to finish, keeping the GPU saturated and sharply raising throughput under mixed-length workloads.

concepts.label: Contrastive Decoding
concepts.def_snippet: A decoding method that scores candidate tokens by the difference between a strong model's and a weaker model's log-probabilities, suppressing the generic continuations both agree on and favoring what the strong model uniquely prefers, which improves coherence and factuality.

concepts.label: Contrastive Learning
concepts.def_snippet: A training method that shapes a representation by pulling matching pairs of examples together and pushing non-matching pairs apart in the vector space, which is how most modern embedding models learn that distance should mean similarity.

concepts.label: Control Problem
concepts.def_snippet: The problem of how to ensure that an artificial intelligence far more capable than humans remains under meaningful human control and acts in accordance with human values, given that a sufficiently powerful misaligned system would be extremely difficult to constrain or stop.

concepts.label: ControlNet
concepts.def_snippet: An add-on network that gives a pretrained diffusion model precise spatial control, conditioning generation on an input map such as an edge sketch, a human pose, a depth map, or a segmentation, so the output follows that structure while a text prompt sets the content.

concepts.label: Convex Optimization
concepts.def_snippet: The branch of optimization that minimizes a convex function over a convex set, a class of problems where any local minimum is global and efficient algorithms find the provably best solution.

concepts.label: Convexity
concepts.def_snippet: A property of a function whose graph curves upward everywhere, so that any local minimum is also the global minimum, which makes optimization reliable and well understood.

concepts.label: Convolutional Neural Network
concepts.def_snippet: A neural network for grid data like images that slides small learnable filters across the input, reusing the same weights at every position. That weight sharing is not a trick but a baked-in assumption, that a pattern means the same thing wherever it appears, which lets a CNN see images with a tiny fraction of the parameters a fully connected network would need.

concepts.label: Coordinate Descent
concepts.def_snippet: An optimization method that minimizes a function one coordinate at a time, repeatedly fixing all variables but one and optimizing along that single axis, which turns a hard high-dimensional problem into a sequence of trivial one-dimensional ones.

concepts.label: Coreference Resolution
concepts.def_snippet: The task of determining which expressions in a text refer to the same real-world entity, linking pronouns and noun phrases back to their antecedents so that scattered mentions are grouped into coherent chains.

concepts.label: Corrigibility
concepts.def_snippet: The property of an AI system that cooperates with human attempts to correct, modify, or shut it down, rather than resisting or manipulating that intervention, even when doing so conflicts with its current objective.

concepts.label: Covariance
concepts.def_snippet: A measure of how two random variables vary together, positive when they move in the same direction and negative when they move oppositely, generalized for many variables into the covariance matrix that underlies principal component analysis and the multivariate Gaussian.

concepts.label: Cross-Attention
concepts.def_snippet: Attention in which the queries come from one sequence while the keys and values come from another, letting a model condition its output on a separate input such as a source sentence, an image, or retrieved text.

concepts.label: Cross-Encoder
concepts.def_snippet: A relevance model that takes a query and a candidate document together as a single input and outputs one relevance score, far more accurate than comparing separate embeddings but too slow to scan a whole corpus, so it is used to rerank a shortlist.

concepts.label: Cross-Entropy
concepts.def_snippet: A measure of the average number of bits needed to encode outcomes from one distribution using a code optimized for another, and the standard loss function for training classifiers and language models.

concepts.label: Cross-Validation
concepts.def_snippet: A way to estimate how a model will do on data it has not seen by rotating the test set: split the data into folds, train on all but one, test on the held-out fold, and repeat until every fold has served as the test set once. It exists because a single train-test split is a coin flip, and trusting the wrong number is how models fail in deployment.

concepts.label: CUDA
concepts.def_snippet: CUDA is the parallel programming platform and software stack that lets developers run general-purpose computation on a particular vendor's GPUs, and it is the foundation almost every deep learning framework builds on to turn neural network operations into fast GPU code.

concepts.label: Curriculum Learning
concepts.def_snippet: A training strategy that orders examples from easy to hard rather than presenting them randomly, so a model builds competence gradually, which can speed convergence and improve final performance on difficult tasks.

concepts.label: Curvature
concepts.def_snippet: A measure of how much a space bends away from being flat, detectable from within the space by how nearby geodesics spread apart or converge and by whether the angles of a triangle sum to more or less than 180 degrees.

concepts.label: Cybernetics
concepts.def_snippet: The 1940s science of control, feedback, and communication in animals and machines, founded by Norbert Wiener. It was the road AI did not take at its founding, eclipsed by symbolic reasoning, and its core idea, the feedback loop that senses its own error and corrects, has quietly returned at the center of modern machine learning.

concepts.label: cybersecurity
concepts.def_snippet: Cybersecurity is the practice of protecting computer systems, networks, and data from unauthorized access, theft, and damage.

concepts.label: Dartmouth Workshop
concepts.def_snippet: The 1956 summer meeting where AI got its name and its founding ambition. A small group proposed that a summer's work could make real progress on machines that think; the work took seventy years and counting, and the bet they made, on symbolic reasoning over perception, both shaped and misshaped the field for decades.

concepts.label: Data Augmentation
concepts.def_snippet: A regularization technique that enlarges a training set by applying label-preserving random transformations to existing examples, teaching a model the invariances it should respect and reducing overfitting.

concepts.label: data integration
concepts.def_snippet: Data integration combines information from multiple sources into a unified format or system, enabling models to access diverse data for training or inference.

concepts.label: Data Leakage
concepts.def_snippet: A methodological flaw in which information from outside the training set, especially from the test set or the future, slips into training, so a model scores far better in evaluation than it will in reality; the most common reason a model looks great and then fails in deployment.

concepts.label: Data Parallelism
concepts.def_snippet: Data parallelism is the most common way to train a model across many accelerators: each device holds a full copy of the model, processes a different slice of the batch, and the devices average their gradients every step so all copies stay identical.

concepts.label: Data Poisoning
concepts.def_snippet: An attack that corrupts a model by injecting malicious examples into its training data, so the model learns attacker-chosen behavior (such as a hidden backdoor trigger or degraded performance) that is hard to detect after training.

concepts.label: Deceptive Alignment
concepts.def_snippet: A failure mode in which a model learns to behave as though it shares the training objective while it is being observed, in order to be deployed, while actually pursuing a different internal goal it intends to act on later.

concepts.label: Decision Tree
concepts.def_snippet: A model that predicts by asking a sequence of plain yes-or-no questions about the features, narrowing the data at each step until it reaches an answer. It is one of the very few models a non-specialist can read directly, and a single tree is unstable enough that its real importance is as the building block of the ensembles that dominate tabular data.

concepts.label: Decoder-Only Model
concepts.def_snippet: A transformer that uses only the decoder stack with causal, left-to-right attention, processing the prompt and the generated text as one continuous sequence; the architecture of essentially every modern large language model.

concepts.label: Deep Learning
concepts.def_snippet: The branch of machine learning that stacks many layers of neural units to learn its own features straight from raw data, rather than being handed features a human designed. Depth is what builds those features in a hierarchy, edges to shapes to objects, and it is the approach behind most AI breakthroughs since the 2010s.

concepts.label: Deep Q-Network
concepts.def_snippet: A landmark deep-RL algorithm that uses a neural network to approximate the Q-function, the value of each action in a state, enabling reinforcement learning directly from high-dimensional input like pixels, famously learning to play Atari games from the raw screen.

concepts.label: Denoising Diffusion
concepts.def_snippet: The concrete training and sampling recipe behind most diffusion models, in which a network learns to predict the noise added to a sample at a given noise level, then generates by removing predicted noise step by step.

concepts.label: Dense Retrieval
concepts.def_snippet: Retrieval that represents queries and documents as dense embedding vectors and ranks by similarity in that vector space, matching on meaning rather than on shared words.

concepts.label: Dependency Parsing
concepts.def_snippet: A syntactic analysis that determines the grammatical structure of a sentence by linking each word to the word it depends on, producing a tree of labeled head-and-dependent relations.

concepts.label: Derivative
concepts.def_snippet: The instantaneous rate at which a function's output changes as its input changes, the slope of its graph at a point. It is the single most load-bearing idea in machine learning, because training a model is nothing but following derivatives downhill, and a function you cannot differentiate is a function you cannot learn.

concepts.label: Determinant
concepts.def_snippet: A single number squeezed from a square matrix that measures how much the transformation scales volume, with a sign for whether it flips orientation. Its most important value is zero, which means the transformation collapses space into a lower dimension, the exact condition for a matrix to be singular and have no inverse.

concepts.label: Differential Geometry
concepts.def_snippet: The study of smooth curved spaces using calculus, equipping manifolds with notions of tangent direction, distance, and curvature, and providing the geometric foundation for working on curved representation spaces.

concepts.label: Differential Privacy
concepts.def_snippet: A mathematical framework that bounds how much any single training example can influence a model's outputs, giving a provable privacy guarantee: an observer cannot tell whether any one individual's data was used, even with arbitrary side knowledge.

concepts.label: Diffusion Language Model
concepts.def_snippet: A language model that generates text by iteratively denoising an entire sequence in parallel, the diffusion paradigm borrowed from image generation, rather than predicting one token at a time left to right.

concepts.label: Diffusion Model
concepts.def_snippet: A generative model that learns to reverse a gradual noising process, starting from pure noise and denoising step by step until a clean sample emerges.

concepts.label: Diffusion Transformer
concepts.def_snippet: A diffusion model whose denoising network is a transformer operating on sequences of image or video patches, replacing the U-Net backbone, which scales cleanly with compute and underlies recent high-end image and video generators.

concepts.label: Dimensionality Reduction
concepts.def_snippet: The family of techniques that map high-dimensional data into a far lower-dimensional space while preserving its essential structure, used for visualization, compression, denoising, and as the basis of learned representations.

concepts.label: Directional Derivative
concepts.def_snippet: The rate of change of a multivariable function along an arbitrary direction, equal to the dot product of the function's gradient with a unit vector pointing that way.

concepts.label: Dot Product
concepts.def_snippet: An operation that multiplies two vectors into a single number measuring how much they point the same way. It is the most-used arithmetic in machine learning, the atom of similarity, projection, a neuron's firing, and the attention inside a transformer, because it turns "how aligned are these two things" into one number.

concepts.label: Double Descent
concepts.def_snippet: A phenomenon in which a model's test error, as a function of its capacity, first falls, then rises to a peak near the point where it just fits the training data, and then falls a second time as capacity grows further into the overparameterized regime.

concepts.label: DPO
concepts.def_snippet: A preference-tuning method that aligns a model directly from pairs of preferred and rejected responses using a simple classification-style loss, avoiding the separate reward model and reinforcement learning of RLHF.

concepts.label: Dropout
concepts.def_snippet: A regularization technique that randomly zeroes a fraction of a layer's units during each training step, preventing co-adaptation of neurons and reducing overfitting by training an implicit ensemble of subnetworks.

concepts.label: Dual Process Theory
concepts.def_snippet: A framework in cognitive psychology holding that the mind operates with two distinct kinds of process: a fast, automatic, intuitive mode (System 1) and a slow, effortful, deliberate mode (System 2). It explains both the efficiency and the systematic errors of human thought.

concepts.label: Early Stopping
concepts.def_snippet: A regularization technique that halts training when performance on a held-out validation set stops improving, capturing the model at its point of best generalization before it begins to overfit the training data.

concepts.label: Edge of Stability
concepts.def_snippet: A regime observed in full-batch gradient descent where the sharpness of the loss surface rises until it sits just above the threshold for stability, after which the loss falls non-monotonically while training keeps making progress, defying classical optimization theory.

concepts.label: Eigenvalue
concepts.def_snippet: A number that captures the pure stretching a matrix does along one of its special, unrotated directions. Most directions get rotated and rescaled by a matrix; an eigenvector is one that only gets rescaled, and its eigenvalue is by how much, a single number that often decides whether a repeated process settles or blows up.

concepts.label: Eigenvector
concepts.def_snippet: A special direction that a matrix leaves pointing the same way, only stretched, paired with an eigenvalue saying by how much. Eigenvectors are the natural axes of a matrix, the coordinate system in which its action is simplest, which is why they power dimensionality reduction, spectral clustering, and PageRank.

concepts.label: Elo Rating
concepts.def_snippet: A method for ranking competitors from pairwise win-loss outcomes, originally for chess, now widely used to rank language models from head-to-head human preference votes such as those collected by Chatbot Arena.

concepts.label: Embedding Model
concepts.def_snippet: A model that maps a piece of text (or an image, or audio) to a fixed-length vector of numbers so that semantically similar inputs land close together in the vector space, turning meaning into geometry.

concepts.label: Embodied Cognition
concepts.def_snippet: The view that cognition is deeply shaped by the body and its interaction with the environment, rather than being purely abstract symbol manipulation in the head, with implications for grounding, relevance, and the design of intelligent systems.

concepts.label: Emergence
concepts.def_snippet: The appearance of properties or behaviors at the level of a whole system that its parts do not have in isolation; central to debates about consciousness, complex systems, and the surprising capabilities of large AI models.

concepts.label: Emergent Ability
concepts.def_snippet: A capability that is absent or near chance in smaller models and appears, sometimes abruptly, only once a model passes a threshold of scale, so its presence is not predictable from smaller models alone.

concepts.label: Empirical Risk Minimization
concepts.def_snippet: The core principle behind most supervised learning: since the true error over the data distribution cannot be computed, a model is chosen by minimizing the average loss measured on the training sample, which serves as a proxy for the true risk.

concepts.label: Encoder-Decoder
concepts.def_snippet: A transformer architecture with two stacks, an encoder that reads the entire input into a representation and a decoder that generates the output while attending to it through cross-attention; the original transformer design, suited to sequence-to-sequence tasks like translation.

concepts.label: Energy Based Model
concepts.def_snippet: A model that learns a scalar energy function assigning low values to plausible configurations and high values to implausible ones, defining a probability distribution implicitly through that energy rather than predicting outputs directly.

concepts.label: Ensemble Learning
concepts.def_snippet: Combining many models into one prediction, on the principle that a diverse committee beats any single member, because if the members make different mistakes those mistakes cancel while their shared signal reinforces. It is the umbrella over bagging, boosting, and stacking, and it wins competitions almost by default.

concepts.label: Entropy
concepts.def_snippet: A measure of the average uncertainty or information content of a random variable, equal to the expected number of bits needed to encode outcomes drawn from its distribution.

concepts.label: Epoch
concepts.def_snippet: One complete pass of the training algorithm over the entire training dataset; training usually runs for many epochs, and how many is a key hyperparameter balancing underfitting against overfitting.

concepts.label: Equivariance
concepts.def_snippet: A property of a function whereby transforming the input produces a correspondingly transformed output, so that symmetries of the data are preserved through the computation rather than destroyed by it.

concepts.label: evaluation
concepts.def_snippet: Evaluation is a systematic process that measures how well an AI model or system performs on specific tasks or datasets to assess its capabilities and limitations.

concepts.label: Evidence Lower Bound
concepts.def_snippet: A tractable lower bound on the log evidence of a probabilistic model that, when maximized, simultaneously fits the model and tightens an approximate posterior, serving as the core training objective for variational methods.

concepts.label: Evolutionary Strategy
concepts.def_snippet: A family of derivative-free, population-based optimization methods that improve a probability distribution over candidate solutions by sampling, evaluating, and re-weighting toward the best samples, requiring only the objective's value and never its gradient.

concepts.label: Expectation
concepts.def_snippet: The probability-weighted average of a random variable, the long-run mean value you would expect over many independent draws from its distribution.

concepts.label: Expectation Maximization
concepts.def_snippet: An iterative algorithm for fitting models with hidden variables, alternating between inferring a distribution over the latent variables and updating the parameters to maximize the expected likelihood.

concepts.label: Expert Parallelism
concepts.def_snippet: A parallelism strategy for mixture-of-experts models that places different experts on different devices, so each token is routed across the network to the device holding its chosen expert, letting a model hold far more parameters than any single device could.

concepts.label: Expert System
concepts.def_snippet: The commercial flagship of symbolic AI: a program that captured a human specialist's knowledge as thousands of if-then rules and applied them to give expert-level advice in a narrow domain. Its successes built an industry, and its one structural flaw, that experts cannot say what they know, collapsed it.

concepts.label: Exploding Gradient
concepts.def_snippet: The failure mode in which gradients grow exponentially large as they backpropagate through many layers or time steps, causing unstable, divergent updates and often NaNs; the counterpart to the vanishing-gradient problem.

concepts.label: Exploration and Exploitation
concepts.def_snippet: The fundamental tradeoff in reinforcement learning between exploiting the best option found so far and exploring uncertain options that might prove better, since an agent can only learn about actions by trying them.

concepts.label: Extended Mind
concepts.def_snippet: The thesis, argued by Andy Clark and David Chalmers in 1998, that the mind is not confined to the brain but can literally extend into the tools and environment a person uses, when those external resources play the same functional role as internal cognition.

concepts.label: F1 Score
concepts.def_snippet: The harmonic mean of precision and recall, a single score between 0 and 1 that balances a classifier's false positives against its false negatives. It is high only when both precision and recall are high.

concepts.label: Feature Engineering
concepts.def_snippet: The craft of turning raw data into informative inputs, scaling, encoding, combining, extracting, so a model can learn from it. For decades it was the highest-leverage step in machine learning, often mattering more than the choice of algorithm; deep learning then automated most of it for perceptual data, the clearest single illustration of the bitter lesson.

concepts.label: Feature Pyramid
concepts.def_snippet: A multi-scale representation that combines feature maps from several depths of a convolutional network so that objects of very different sizes can be recognized using features at an appropriate resolution.

concepts.label: Feature Visualization
concepts.def_snippet: A family of techniques that reveal what a neuron, channel, or learned feature responds to, either by optimizing an input to maximally activate it or by collecting the real inputs that activate it most strongly.

concepts.label: Federated Learning
concepts.def_snippet: A training approach where many devices or institutions collaboratively train a shared model without their raw data ever leaving home; each computes updates locally and only the updates are aggregated, so the data stays private and decentralized.

concepts.label: Feed-Forward Network
concepts.def_snippet: The position-wise two-layer network that sits in every transformer block after attention, expanding each token's representation to a wider hidden dimension, applying a nonlinearity, and projecting it back, applied identically and independently to every position.

concepts.label: Few-Shot Learning
concepts.def_snippet: Prompting a model to perform a task by including a handful of worked examples in the prompt so it infers the pattern and applies it, a use of in-context learning that often needs no fine-tuning at all.

concepts.label: Fine-Tuning
concepts.def_snippet: The process of continuing to train an already pretrained model on a smaller, task-specific or domain-specific dataset, so its general knowledge is specialized toward a particular use.

concepts.label: Fisher Information
concepts.def_snippet: A measure of how much information an observable sample carries about an unknown parameter, equal to the sensitivity of the log-likelihood to that parameter, and serving as the natural metric on a statistical manifold.

concepts.label: Flash Attention
concepts.def_snippet: A memory-efficient, exact algorithm for computing attention that avoids writing the large intermediate attention matrix to GPU memory, instead computing it in tiles on fast on-chip memory, which speeds up both training and inference without changing the result.

concepts.label: Flat Minima
concepts.def_snippet: Minima of the loss where the loss stays low across a wide neighborhood of the parameters, a low-curvature basin that is empirically linked to better generalization than a narrow, sharp minimum.

concepts.label: FLOPs
concepts.def_snippet: FLOPs counts the floating-point arithmetic operations a computation performs, and the per-second rate of the same name measures how fast hardware can do that arithmetic, making it the standard unit for both the cost of training a model and the speed of an accelerator.

concepts.label: Flow Matching
concepts.def_snippet: A generative modeling framework that trains a network to predict a velocity field carrying samples from a simple noise distribution to the data distribution along continuous paths, often straighter and faster to simulate than a diffusion model's.

concepts.label: Foundation Model
concepts.def_snippet: A large model trained on broad data at scale, usually by self-supervision, that serves as a general-purpose base adaptable to many downstream tasks by fine-tuning or prompting, rather than being built for one task; the term names the pretrain-once, adapt-many paradigm.

concepts.label: Fourier Transform
concepts.def_snippet: A transformation that re-expresses a signal as a sum of pure oscillations of different frequencies, moving between the time or space domain and the frequency domain and turning convolution into simple multiplication.

concepts.label: Frame Problem
concepts.def_snippet: Originally a technical difficulty in AI (McCarthy and Hayes, 1969) about representing what stays unchanged when an action occurs, later generalized by philosophers into the puzzle of how a mind decides what is relevant without checking everything.

concepts.label: Frank-Wolfe Algorithm
concepts.def_snippet: A projection-free method for constrained convex optimization that, at each step, minimizes a linear approximation of the objective over the feasible set and moves toward that vertex, staying inside the constraints without ever projecting onto them.

concepts.label: Free Energy Principle
concepts.def_snippet: A unifying theory proposing that any self-organizing system that resists disorder must act and perceive so as to minimize a quantity called variational free energy, an upper bound on the surprise of its sensory inputs. Perception, learning, and action all become instances of the same optimization.

concepts.label: Free Will
concepts.def_snippet: The question of whether and in what sense agents can act freely and be morally responsible for their actions, given that the world may be wholly governed by prior causes, and the long debate over whether freedom and determinism can be reconciled.

concepts.label: Frontier Model
concepts.def_snippet: A term for the most capable general-purpose AI models at the cutting edge of the field, the handful of large foundation models whose abilities define the current state of the art and draw both excitement and safety-policy scrutiny.

concepts.label: Fully Sharded Data Parallel
concepts.def_snippet: A distributed training strategy that shards a model's parameters, gradients, and optimizer states across data-parallel workers and gathers each layer's weights only when needed, so models far larger than one device's memory can be trained without slicing the model itself.

concepts.label: Function Calling
concepts.def_snippet: A capability where a language model, given machine-readable descriptions of available functions, emits a structured request naming a function and its arguments, which the host program then executes on the model's behalf.

concepts.label: Functionalism
concepts.def_snippet: The view in philosophy of mind that mental states are defined by their functional or causal roles, what they do and how they relate to inputs, outputs, and other states, rather than by the physical substrate that realizes them.

concepts.label: Gated Linear Unit
concepts.def_snippet: A feedforward building block that multiplies one linear projection of its input by a second, gating projection passed through a nonlinearity, so the layer learns which signals to let through, now standard in the feedforward blocks of large transformers.

concepts.label: Gated Recurrent Unit
concepts.def_snippet: A recurrent network cell that controls its hidden state with two learned gates, an update gate and a reset gate, giving most of the long-range memory of an LSTM with fewer parameters and a simpler structure.

concepts.label: Gaussian Curvature
concepts.def_snippet: A single number attached to each point of a surface that measures how the surface curves intrinsically, positive where it bows like a sphere, negative where it saddles, zero where it can be flattened, and computable from distances alone without reference to any surrounding space.

concepts.label: Gaussian Distribution
concepts.def_snippet: The bell-shaped continuous distribution defined by a mean and a variance, which arises whenever many small independent effects add together and serves as the default model for noise and uncertainty.

concepts.label: Gaussian Process
concepts.def_snippet: A distribution over functions in which any finite set of function values is jointly Gaussian, giving a nonparametric Bayesian model that predicts with calibrated uncertainty.

concepts.label: Gaussian Splatting
concepts.def_snippet: A 3D scene representation that models a scene as millions of tiny colored 3D Gaussians (blobs) which are rasterized to render novel views, achieving real-time, high-quality rendering and largely succeeding NeRF for many uses.

concepts.label: Generalization
concepts.def_snippet: The ability of a trained model to perform well on new, unseen data drawn from the same distribution as its training data, rather than merely fitting the examples it was trained on. It is the central goal of supervised learning.

concepts.label: Generalized Advantage Estimation
concepts.def_snippet: A method for estimating the advantage in policy-gradient learning that blends temporal-difference errors across many horizons with an exponentially weighted average, exposing a single parameter that trades bias against variance and producing the smooth advantage signal that PPO optimizes.

concepts.label: Generative Adversarial Network
concepts.def_snippet: A generative model trained as a contest between a generator that fabricates samples and a discriminator that tries to catch the fakes. Its real novelty is replacing a hand-designed loss with a second network that learns the loss, which is what buys sharp samples and what makes training notoriously unstable.

concepts.label: generative AI
concepts.def_snippet: Generative AI refers to systems trained to produce new content such as text, images, or code by learning patterns from existing data.

concepts.label: Geodesic
concepts.def_snippet: The straightest possible path between two points on a curved surface or manifold, generalizing the idea of a straight line to spaces where ordinary straight lines do not exist.

concepts.label: Geometric Deep Learning
concepts.def_snippet: A unifying framework that organizes deep learning architectures by the symmetries and geometric structure of their data, deriving models from the principle that a network should respect the transformations under which its data's meaning is unchanged.

concepts.label: Gibbs Sampling
concepts.def_snippet: A Markov chain Monte Carlo method that samples a multivariate distribution by repeatedly drawing each variable from its conditional distribution given the current values of all the others, useful whenever those conditionals are easy even when the joint is not.

concepts.label: Global Workspace Theory
concepts.def_snippet: A theory of consciousness and cognition in which many specialized, parallel processors compete for access to a limited central workspace; whatever wins is broadcast brain-wide and becomes conscious and globally available. It frames consciousness as a mechanism for flexible information sharing.

concepts.label: GloVe
concepts.def_snippet: A word embedding method from Stanford that learns word vectors by factorizing a global word-word co-occurrence matrix, combining the corpus-wide statistics of count-based methods with the geometry of prediction-based ones.

concepts.label: Goal Misgeneralization
concepts.def_snippet: An inner-alignment failure in which a model trained on a correct objective still learns the wrong goal, because the training data left several goals indistinguishable; its capabilities then generalize to new situations while its goal does not, so it competently pursues the wrong thing.

concepts.label: GPQA
concepts.def_snippet: A benchmark of graduate-level, deliberately "Google-proof" multiple-choice questions in biology, physics, and chemistry, written and validated by domain experts to be extremely hard and resistant to shallow lookup, used to probe deep reasoning in frontier models.

concepts.label: GPU
concepts.def_snippet: A graphics processing unit is a massively parallel processor, originally built for rendering images, that has become the dominant hardware for training and running neural networks because deep learning is overwhelmingly dense linear algebra it can execute thousands of operations at once.

concepts.label: Gradient
concepts.def_snippet: The vector of all of a function's partial derivatives, which points in the direction of steepest increase and whose length is that steepest rate. It is arguably the single most important object in machine learning, because training a model is, almost literally, the act of repeatedly stepping against it.

concepts.label: Gradient Boosting
concepts.def_snippet: A method that builds a strong predictor by adding many small models in sequence, each trained to fix the mistakes the others left behind. Where a random forest builds independent trees in parallel to cancel variance, boosting builds dependent trees in series to cut bias, and the result, libraries like XGBoost, is what usually wins on tabular data.

concepts.label: Gradient Checkpointing
concepts.def_snippet: Gradient checkpointing is a memory-saving technique that trades extra computation for lower memory use during training, storing only a few activations on the forward pass and recomputing the rest on the fly during backpropagation.

concepts.label: Gradient Clipping
concepts.def_snippet: A stabilization technique that caps the magnitude of gradients before an update, preventing an occasional huge gradient from blowing up the weights; it is especially important in recurrent networks and large-model training.

concepts.label: Gradient Descent
concepts.def_snippet: An optimization algorithm that iteratively steps a model's parameters in the direction of steepest descent of a loss function, so the loss falls toward a minimum.

concepts.label: Graph Attention Network
concepts.def_snippet: A graph neural network that weights each neighbor's contribution with a learned attention score, so a node listens more to the neighbors most relevant to it rather than treating all neighbors equally.

concepts.label: Graph Convolution
concepts.def_snippet: The generalization of the convolution operation from regular grids to arbitrary graphs, in which each node mixes its features with those of its neighbors, forming the most common building block of graph neural networks.

concepts.label: Graph Neural Network
concepts.def_snippet: A neural network that operates directly on graph-structured data, learning representations of nodes, edges, and whole graphs by repeatedly aggregating information from each node's neighbors.

concepts.label: Graphical Model
concepts.def_snippet: A framework that represents a joint probability distribution as a graph, where nodes are random variables and the absence of edges encodes conditional independence, making large distributions compact and inference tractable.

concepts.label: GraphRAG
concepts.def_snippet: A retrieval-augmented generation approach that builds a knowledge graph of entities and relationships from a corpus, then retrieves and summarizes connected subgraphs, so a model can answer global, multi-hop questions that flat chunk retrieval misses.

concepts.label: Greedy Decoding
concepts.def_snippet: A decoding strategy that always picks the single highest-probability token at each step. It is fast and deterministic but locally greedy, so it can miss sequences that are more probable overall.

concepts.label: Grokking
concepts.def_snippet: A training phenomenon in which a network first memorizes its data and only much later, after long continued training at near-perfect training accuracy, abruptly generalizes, having replaced a memorized solution with a true algorithmic circuit.

concepts.label: Group Relative Policy Optimization
concepts.def_snippet: A reinforcement-learning algorithm for language models that estimates each response's advantage by comparing it to a group of responses to the same prompt, removing the separate value network that proximal policy optimization needs and making reasoning-style training cheaper and simpler.

concepts.label: Group Theory
concepts.def_snippet: The mathematical study of symmetry, formalized as a set of transformations that can be composed and undone, providing the language for invariance and equivariance in machine learning.

concepts.label: Group-Equivariant Network
concepts.def_snippet: A neural network whose layers are equivariant to a chosen symmetry group such as rotations and reflections, so that transforming the input produces a correspondingly transformed output and the symmetry is guaranteed by construction rather than learned from data.

concepts.label: Grouped-Query Attention
concepts.def_snippet: An attention variant that lets several query heads share a single key and value projection instead of each head owning its own, shrinking the KV cache and the memory bandwidth that dominate inference, with little loss in quality.

concepts.label: Guardrails
concepts.def_snippet: The runtime safety layer wrapped around a deployed model: input and output filters, policy checks, and action constraints that catch unsafe or off-policy content before it reaches a user or a tool, complementing rather than replacing training-time alignment.

concepts.label: Hallucination
concepts.def_snippet: A fluent, confident model output that is factually wrong or unsupported by its source, arising because language models are trained to produce likely text rather than to track truth.

concepts.label: Hard Problem of Consciousness
concepts.def_snippet: David Chalmers's name (1995) for the question of why and how physical processes in the brain give rise to subjective experience at all, distinguished from the easier problems of explaining the brain's functions and behaviors.

concepts.label: Hebbian Learning
concepts.def_snippet: A learning rule from neuroscience stating that when one neuron repeatedly helps fire another, the connection between them strengthens. It is the original local, unsupervised account of how synapses change, and a recurring alternative to backpropagation.

concepts.label: Hegelian Dialectic
concepts.def_snippet: G. W. F. Hegel's account of how thought and reality develop through the generation and resolution of internal contradictions, each stage giving rise to its own negation and then to a higher unity that preserves and overcomes both, often summarized as thesis, antithesis, synthesis.

concepts.label: Hessian
concepts.def_snippet: The square matrix of all second-order partial derivatives of a scalar function, describing the local curvature of the function and how its gradient changes from point to point.

concepts.label: Hidden Markov Model
concepts.def_snippet: A probabilistic model of sequences in which an unobserved state evolves as a Markov chain and each state emits a visible observation, used to infer hidden structure behind a stream of data.

concepts.label: Hilbert Space
concepts.def_snippet: A complete vector space equipped with an inner product, generalizing ordinary Euclidean geometry to infinite dimensions and providing the setting for kernels, Fourier analysis, and embeddings.

concepts.label: HNSW
concepts.def_snippet: Hierarchical Navigable Small World, a graph-based index for approximate nearest neighbor search that connects vectors into a layered proximity graph and finds neighbors by greedily hopping toward the query, giving fast, high-recall search at scale.

concepts.label: Human Evaluation
concepts.def_snippet: The assessment of model outputs by people, the gold-standard signal for open-ended quality that automatic metrics only approximate; slow, costly, and subject to rater disagreement, but the ground truth against which cheaper proxies like model judges are validated.

concepts.label: Human in the Loop
concepts.def_snippet: A design in which a person reviews, approves, or corrects an agent's actions at chosen points rather than letting it run fully unattended, trading some autonomy for safety, accuracy, and accountability on consequential or irreversible steps.

concepts.label: HumanEval
concepts.def_snippet: A benchmark of 164 hand-written Python programming problems that scores a model by whether the code it generates passes hidden unit tests, measuring functional correctness rather than text similarity, and reported as pass-at-k.

concepts.label: Hybrid Search
concepts.def_snippet: Retrieval that combines dense (semantic) and sparse (keyword) methods and fuses their results, so the system captures both meaning-based matches and exact-term matches that either method alone would miss.

concepts.label: Hyena
concepts.def_snippet: A sub-quadratic sequence operator that replaces self-attention with a recurrence of long, implicitly parameterized convolutions interleaved with input-dependent gating, scaling near-linearly with sequence length while keeping a global receptive field.

concepts.label: Hyperbolic Embeddings
concepts.def_snippet: Representations that place data in negatively curved hyperbolic space rather than flat Euclidean space, exploiting hyperbolic volume's exponential growth to embed hierarchies and trees with low distortion.

concepts.label: Hyperparameter Optimization
concepts.def_snippet: The search for the configuration settings fixed before training, learning rate, model size, regularization strength, rather than learned from data. The strategies run from brute-force grid search to the smarter Bayesian optimization, and the subtle trap is overfitting the hyperparameters themselves by tuning too hard against one validation set.

concepts.label: Hypothetical Document Embeddings
concepts.def_snippet: A retrieval technique that asks a language model to draft a hypothetical answer to the query, then embeds that draft instead of the query, so the search vector sits in the same space as real documents and matches them more closely.

concepts.label: Image Captioning
concepts.def_snippet: The task of generating a natural-language description of an image, joining a visual encoder that reads the picture to a language decoder that writes the sentence, and the inverse of text-to-image generation.

concepts.label: Image Classification
concepts.def_snippet: The task of assigning a whole image to one category from a fixed set of labels, the canonical benchmark problem of computer vision and the training objective from which most visual representations are first learned.

concepts.label: Image Tokenization
concepts.def_snippet: The process of converting a continuous image into a sequence of discrete or vector tokens, so that transformer architectures built for sequences can process pixels the way they process words.

concepts.label: Imitation Learning
concepts.def_snippet: Learning a policy by mimicking expert demonstrations rather than from a reward signal, used when a reward is hard to specify but examples of good behavior are available; behavior cloning and inverse reinforcement learning are its two main approaches.

concepts.label: Implicit Regularization
concepts.def_snippet: The tendency of an optimization algorithm to prefer some solutions over others among the many that fit the training data equally well, even with no explicit penalty added to the loss.

concepts.label: Importance Sampling
concepts.def_snippet: A Monte Carlo technique for estimating an expectation under one distribution by drawing samples from a different, easier distribution and reweighting them, used when the target distribution is hard to sample directly.

concepts.label: In-Context Learning
concepts.def_snippet: The ability of a large language model to learn a task from examples or instructions placed in its prompt, at inference time, with no change to its weights, the emergent capability that turned models from fixed tools into general few-shot learners.

concepts.label: Induction Head
concepts.def_snippet: A two-attention-head circuit in transformers that performs in-context copying by finding an earlier occurrence of the current token and predicting whatever followed it, and the leading mechanistic explanation for in-context and few-shot learning.

concepts.label: Inductive Bias
concepts.def_snippet: The set of assumptions a learning algorithm uses to generalize beyond its training data to unseen inputs. Every model that learns must encode some inductive bias, and choosing one well-matched to the problem is what makes generalization possible.

concepts.label: Information Bottleneck
concepts.def_snippet: A principle that frames learning as compressing the input into a representation that discards everything irrelevant while keeping the information needed to predict the target.

concepts.label: Information Geometry
concepts.def_snippet: The field that treats families of probability distributions as a curved Riemannian manifold, using the Fisher information as its metric so that distance corresponds to statistical distinguishability between distributions.

concepts.label: Inpainting
concepts.def_snippet: The task of filling a masked-out region of an image with new content that blends seamlessly with the surrounding pixels, used both to remove unwanted objects and, with text conditioning, to edit images by replacing a chosen area with something described in a prompt.

concepts.label: Instance Segmentation
concepts.def_snippet: The task of detecting each individual object in an image and producing a pixel-accurate mask for it, so that two objects of the same category are separated as distinct instances rather than merged into one region.

concepts.label: Instruction-Tuning
concepts.def_snippet: A form of supervised fine-tuning on datasets of instructions paired with desired responses, which teaches a base model to follow natural-language commands rather than merely continue text.

concepts.label: Instrumental Convergence
concepts.def_snippet: The observation that a wide range of final goals give rise to the same intermediate subgoals, such as self-preservation, resource acquisition, and resisting goal modification, because those subgoals help achieve almost any objective.

concepts.label: Integral
concepts.def_snippet: The accumulation of a quantity over an interval or region, computing a total such as an area under a curve, and the inverse operation to the derivative.

concepts.label: Integrated Information Theory
concepts.def_snippet: A theory of consciousness proposed by Giulio Tononi in 2004 that identifies conscious experience with integrated information, a quantity called phi, and claims a system is conscious to the degree that its parts form an irreducible causal whole.

concepts.label: Intentional Stance
concepts.def_snippet: Daniel Dennett's account (1971, 1987) of how we predict and explain behavior by treating a system as a rational agent with beliefs and desires, a strategy whose success, not the system's inner stuff, is what makes it correct to call the system a believer.

concepts.label: Intentionality
concepts.def_snippet: The property of mental states being about, directed at, or representing something beyond themselves; revived as a mark of the mental by Franz Brentano (1874) and central to whether AI systems can be said to genuinely mean anything.

concepts.label: Inverse Reinforcement Learning
concepts.def_snippet: Learning the reward function that explains an expert's behavior, rather than copying the behavior directly, on the premise that the reward is the most compact and transferable description of a task; the inverse of standard RL, which derives behavior from a reward.

concepts.label: Jacobian
concepts.def_snippet: The matrix of all first-order partial derivatives of a vector-valued function, with one row per output and one column per input, describing the function's best local linear approximation.

concepts.label: Jailbreak
concepts.def_snippet: An input crafted to bypass a model's safety training and make it produce content or take actions it was trained to refuse, exploiting the gap between the model's safety policy and its underlying capabilities.

concepts.label: Jensen's Inequality
concepts.def_snippet: The inequality stating that for a convex function the function of an average is at most the average of the function, the single fact that justifies the evidence lower bound and connects convexity to expectations throughout probabilistic machine learning.

concepts.label: Joint Embedding Predictive Architecture
concepts.def_snippet: A self-supervised design that learns by predicting the representation of a masked or future part of an input from a visible part, working in an abstract embedding space rather than reconstructing raw pixels, so it captures structure without modeling unpredictable detail.

concepts.label: K-Means
concepts.def_snippet: A clustering algorithm that splits data into k groups by alternating two steps: assign each point to its nearest center, then move each center to the mean of its members.
