It is fast and ubiquitous, and the simplicity hides a strong assumption, that clusters are round, separate blobs of similar size.

concepts.label: K-Nearest Neighbors
concepts.def_snippet: About the most direct idea in machine learning: to label a new point, find the handful of most similar stored examples and let them vote. It does no real training, the dataset itself is the model, and that simple idea is exactly what powers modern embeddings and vector search, nearest-neighbor lookup in a learned space.

concepts.label: Kalman Filter
concepts.def_snippet: A recursive algorithm that estimates the hidden state of a linear dynamical system from noisy measurements, maintaining a Gaussian belief that it updates optimally as each new observation arrives.

concepts.label: Kernel Fusion
concepts.def_snippet: A performance optimization that merges several elementary tensor operations into a single GPU kernel so intermediate results stay in fast on-chip memory instead of being written to and read back from main memory, cutting memory traffic that dominates the runtime of memory-bound operations.

concepts.label: Kernel Method
concepts.def_snippet: A way to give a linear algorithm nonlinear power without paying for it. Many models depend on the data only through similarities between pairs of points; a kernel computes the similarity those points would have in a vastly higher-dimensional space, sometimes infinite-dimensional, directly from the originals, so the method draws curved boundaries while its math stays linear.

concepts.label: KL Divergence
concepts.def_snippet: A measure of how much one probability distribution differs from another, equal to the expected extra bits incurred by encoding data from the true distribution using a model distribution, always non-negative and zero only when the two distributions match.

concepts.label: knowledge bases
concepts.def_snippet: Knowledge bases are structured repositories of information organized to enable efficient retrieval and reasoning, often used in AI systems to ground model outputs in factual data o…

concepts.label: Knowledge Distillation
concepts.def_snippet: A technique for compressing knowledge from a large teacher model into a smaller student model by training the student to imitate the teacher's outputs rather than learning only from hard labels.

concepts.label: Kolmogorov Arnold Network
concepts.def_snippet: A neural network that places learnable nonlinear functions on the connections between nodes instead of fixed activation functions on the nodes, drawing on the Kolmogorov-Arnold representation theorem, which can yield more interpretable and parameter-efficient models on structured problems.

concepts.label: KTO
concepts.def_snippet: A preference-alignment method that tunes a model from individual responses each labeled simply as desirable or undesirable, using a loss inspired by prospect theory, so it needs no paired comparisons and tolerates imbalanced thumbs-up and thumbs-down feedback.

concepts.label: KV Cache
concepts.def_snippet: An inference optimization that stores the key and value vectors of already-processed tokens so a transformer generating text does not recompute them for every new token, trading memory for a large speedup.

concepts.label: L-BFGS
concepts.def_snippet: A limited-memory quasi-Newton optimizer that approximates the inverse Hessian from a short history of recent gradients, gaining much of Newton's method's fast convergence at a memory cost only a few times that of gradient descent.

concepts.label: Lagrange Multiplier
concepts.def_snippet: A method for optimizing a function subject to constraints by introducing a new variable for each constraint, converting a constrained problem into an unconstrained stationarity condition and revealing the price of each constraint.

concepts.label: Large Language Model
concepts.def_snippet: A transformer with billions of parameters trained on vast text corpora by next-token prediction, which acquires broad language and reasoning ability that can be steered to many tasks without task-specific training.

concepts.label: large language models
concepts.def_snippet: Large language models are neural networks trained on vast text corpora to predict and generate human language by processing tokens sequentially through transformer architectures.

concepts.label: Late Interaction
concepts.def_snippet: A retrieval method that encodes a query and a document into per-token embeddings and scores relevance by summing each query token's best match against the document tokens, keeping fine-grained term interactions that a single pooled vector discards.

concepts.label: Latent Diffusion
concepts.def_snippet: A diffusion model that runs in the compressed latent space of an autoencoder rather than on raw pixels, drastically cutting compute while preserving quality, the design behind most large text-to-image systems.

concepts.label: Latent Semantic Analysis
concepts.def_snippet: An early method that uncovers latent topics in a collection of documents by applying singular value decomposition to a term-document matrix, mapping words and documents into a shared low-dimensional semantic space.

concepts.label: Latent Space
concepts.def_snippet: The compressed, lower-dimensional space of learned representations into which a model maps its inputs, where proximity encodes similarity and directions often correspond to interpretable features.

concepts.label: Latent Variable Model
concepts.def_snippet: A probabilistic model that explains observed data through hidden, unobserved variables, positing a compact underlying cause that generates the visible data and must be inferred rather than measured.

concepts.label: Layer Normalization
concepts.def_snippet: A normalization technique that standardizes the activations across the features of a single example to zero mean and unit variance, then rescales them, giving batch-independent normalization that suits sequence models and transformers.

concepts.label: Learning Rate
concepts.def_snippet: The scalar that controls how large a step gradient descent takes at each update, scaling the gradient before it is subtracted from the parameters. It is the single most important hyperparameter in training.

concepts.label: Learning Rate Schedule
concepts.def_snippet: A rule that changes the learning rate over the course of training rather than holding it fixed, typically starting larger for fast early progress and shrinking toward the end so the optimizer can settle precisely into a minimum.

concepts.label: Learning Rate Warmup
concepts.def_snippet: A training technique that begins with a very small learning rate and ramps it up to the target value over the first hundreds or thousands of steps, stabilizing the early phase of training when gradients and adaptive statistics are still unreliable.

concepts.label: Lemmatization
concepts.def_snippet: The process of reducing an inflected word to its canonical dictionary form, its lemma, using vocabulary and grammatical analysis, so that different surface forms of the same word are treated as one.

concepts.label: Lie Group
concepts.def_snippet: A group that is also a smooth manifold, so its symmetry operations form a continuum that can be differentiated, providing the mathematics of continuous symmetry behind rotation-equivariant and other geometric deep learning.

concepts.label: Linear Attention
concepts.def_snippet: A reformulation of self-attention that removes the softmax so the computation can be reordered into a running summary, reducing the cost from quadratic to linear in sequence length and turning the attention layer into a recurrence.

concepts.label: Linear Regression
concepts.def_snippet: The simplest supervised model, fitting a straight-line relationship between inputs and a continuous target by minimizing squared error. Its importance is not as a cutting-edge tool but as the skeleton inside almost everything more elaborate: understand its one idea and you understand the bones of logistic regression and the neural network alike.

concepts.label: Linear Representation Hypothesis
concepts.def_snippet: The hypothesis that neural networks encode high-level concepts as linear directions in activation space, so that features can be read with linear probes, added and removed by vector arithmetic, and combined to form the representations the model computes with.

concepts.label: Linear Transformation
concepts.def_snippet: A function between vector spaces that preserves addition and scaling, which forces a rigid geometry: it can rotate, scale, shear, and project, but it can never bend a straight line or move the origin. Every such map is a matrix, and a stack of them is exactly a neural network layer, which is why the nonlinearity between layers is essential.

concepts.label: Lion Optimizer
concepts.def_snippet: A memory-efficient optimizer, discovered by automated program search, that updates each parameter using only the sign of a momentum-smoothed gradient, tracking one running average instead of the two that Adam maintains.

concepts.label: LLM
concepts.def_snippet: A large language model is a neural network trained on vast text data to predict and generate human language, forming the foundation for most current AI assistants.

concepts.label: LLM as Judge
concepts.def_snippet: Using a strong language model to grade the outputs of other models, either by scoring a single response against a rubric or by choosing the better of two. A scalable proxy for human judgment on open-ended tasks that have no fixed answer key.

concepts.label: LLMs
concepts.def_snippet: Large language models are AI systems trained on vast text data to predict and generate human language, raising questions about their capabilities, limitations, and societal impact.

concepts.label: LM Evaluation Harness
concepts.def_snippet: A widely used open-source framework that standardizes how language models are evaluated across hundreds of benchmarks, fixing prompt formats, few-shot setups, and scoring so results are reproducible and comparable across models.

concepts.label: Local Attractor Basin
concepts.def_snippet: The region of the loss landscape surrounding a local minimum from which a descent-based optimizer flows into that minimum. Its size and flatness determine which solution training reaches and how well that solution generalizes.

concepts.label: local inference
concepts.def_snippet: Local inference is running AI models on a user's own device or server rather than sending data to remote cloud services.

concepts.label: Local Minimum
concepts.def_snippet: A point in the loss landscape that is lower than every point in its immediate neighborhood but not necessarily the lowest point overall, where the gradient vanishes and a descent-based optimizer can come to rest.

concepts.label: Logistic Regression
concepts.def_snippet: A linear model for classification that runs a weighted sum of features through a sigmoid or softmax to produce class probabilities, trained by minimizing cross-entropy. It is a workhorse baseline, and more than that, it is the final layer of almost every neural classifier, so understanding it is understanding what a deep network does in its last step.

concepts.label: Logit Bias
concepts.def_snippet: An inference control that adds a fixed offset to the logits of specified tokens before sampling, nudging the model toward or away from particular words or symbols without retraining, used to ban tokens, steer vocabulary, or shape outputs.

concepts.label: Logit Lens
concepts.def_snippet: An interpretability technique that reads a transformer's intermediate predictions by projecting hidden states from each layer through the model's output head, revealing how its guess about the next token forms and changes across depth.

concepts.label: Logits
concepts.def_snippet: The raw, unnormalized scores a model emits for every token in its vocabulary at each step, before a softmax converts them into a probability distribution. Every decoding and sampling control operates on logits.

concepts.label: Long Context
concepts.def_snippet: A model's ability to process and use very long inputs, from tens of thousands to millions of tokens, enabling whole-document and multi-document reasoning, while straining the quadratic cost of attention, the size of the KV cache, and the model's ability to actually attend across the full window.

concepts.label: Long Short-Term Memory
concepts.def_snippet: A recurrent network built around a protected cell state that information can ride along almost untouched, with learned input, forget, and output gates deciding what to write, keep, and read. The design exists to give the gradient a route through time that is added to rather than repeatedly multiplied, the multiplication that destroys memory in a plain recurrent network.

concepts.label: LoRA
concepts.def_snippet: A parameter-efficient fine-tuning method that freezes a pretrained model and injects small, trainable low-rank weight matrices, so a large model is adapted by training a tiny fraction of its parameters.

concepts.label: Loss Function
concepts.def_snippet: A function that measures how wrong a model's predictions are by mapping its parameters to a single number, the quantity that training tries to minimize. It defines what "good" means for the model.

concepts.label: Loss Landscape
concepts.def_snippet: The high-dimensional surface formed by plotting a model's loss as a function of its parameters. Training is a walk over this terrain, and its geometry, the valleys, ridges, and plateaus, decides how hard optimization is.

concepts.label: Loss of Plasticity
concepts.def_snippet: The gradual erosion of a neural network's ability to learn new things during continued training, where a network that learns well at first becomes progressively unable to fit new data, even though its capacity is unchanged.

concepts.label: Lottery Ticket Hypothesis
concepts.def_snippet: The conjecture that a randomly initialized dense network contains a small subnetwork which, trained in isolation from its original initialization, can match the full network's accuracy.

concepts.label: Machine Learning
concepts.def_snippet: A branch of artificial intelligence in which a system improves at a task by fitting patterns in data instead of following hand-written rules. It answers a specific obstacle: most of what we know how to do, we cannot say how we do, so we supply examples and let the program recover the rule.

concepts.label: Machine Translation
concepts.def_snippet: The automatic translation of text from one natural language to another, the task whose pursuit drove the encoder-decoder architecture, the attention mechanism, and ultimately the transformer.

concepts.label: Mamba
concepts.def_snippet: A selective state space model that makes the recurrence input-dependent, letting the model choose what to remember and what to ignore at each step, and that runs in linear time with a hardware-aware parallel scan instead of quadratic self-attention.

concepts.label: Manifold
concepts.def_snippet: A space that looks flat and ordinary when you zoom in on any small piece of it, even though its global shape may be curved or twisted, generalizing curves and surfaces to any number of dimensions.

concepts.label: Manifold Hypothesis
concepts.def_snippet: The widely held conjecture that real high-dimensional data such as images, audio, and text concentrates on or near a low-dimensional manifold inside its ambient space, rather than filling that space uniformly.

concepts.label: Manifold Optimization
concepts.def_snippet: Optimization in which the variables are constrained to lie on a smooth manifold, performed by taking gradient steps along the manifold's own geometry so that every iterate satisfies the constraint exactly rather than by penalizing or projecting after the fact.

concepts.label: market dynamics
concepts.def_snippet: Market dynamics in AI discourse refers to questions about how competitive positioning, funding, valuation, and business model viability will reshape the AI industry landscape.

concepts.label: Markov Chain
concepts.def_snippet: A sequence of random states in which the next state depends only on the current one and not on the full history, the memoryless model whose transition and stationary distributions underlie sampling algorithms, language models, and reinforcement learning.

concepts.label: Markov Chain Monte Carlo
concepts.def_snippet: A family of algorithms that sample from a complex probability distribution by simulating a Markov chain whose long-run equilibrium is that distribution, used to approximate otherwise intractable Bayesian posteriors and expectations.

concepts.label: Markov Decision Process
concepts.def_snippet: The mathematical framework that formalizes sequential decision making under uncertainty, defined by states, actions, transition probabilities, and rewards, and serving as the standard model on which reinforcement learning is built.

concepts.label: Masked Autoencoder
concepts.def_snippet: A self-supervised pretraining method for vision that masks out most of an image's patches and trains a model to reconstruct the missing content, learning rich visual representations from unlabeled images without any human annotation.

concepts.label: Masked Language Modeling
concepts.def_snippet: A self-supervised pretraining objective that hides a fraction of the tokens in a text and trains a model to predict them from the surrounding context on both sides, producing deeply bidirectional representations.

concepts.label: mathematical reasoning
concepts.def_snippet: Whether AI systems can learn to solve complex mathematical problems through symbolic reasoning or learned patterns, and whether this represents genuine understanding versus statist…

concepts.label: Matrix
concepts.def_snippet: A rectangular grid of numbers best understood not as a table but as an action: feed it a vector and it hands back a transformed vector. That dual life, data when stored and a function when applied, is what makes the matrix the workhorse object of machine learning.

concepts.label: Matrix Multiplication
concepts.def_snippet: The operation that combines two matrices by taking dot products of rows with columns. The strange rule is exactly right because it composes functions: multiplying two matrices builds the single matrix that applies one transformation after the other, and because a neural network is a chain of these products, it is the operation most of the world's compute is spent on.

concepts.label: Matryoshka Embeddings
concepts.def_snippet: An embedding training method that packs information into a vector in order of importance, so a single high-dimensional embedding can be truncated to a shorter prefix and still work, letting one model serve many embedding sizes and enabling cheap coarse-to-fine retrieval.

concepts.label: Maximal Marginal Relevance
concepts.def_snippet: A selection method that builds a result set one item at a time, each step choosing the candidate that is most relevant to the query while least redundant with the items already chosen, so the returned set is both on-topic and diverse instead of a cluster of near-duplicates.

concepts.label: Maximum Likelihood Estimation
concepts.def_snippet: A method for fitting a model's parameters by choosing the values that make the observed data most probable under the model, equivalent to minimizing cross-entropy and to minimizing the KL divergence from the model to the data.

concepts.label: Measure Theory
concepts.def_snippet: The branch of mathematics that assigns a consistent notion of size, length, area, volume, or probability to sets, providing the rigorous foundation on which modern probability and integration are built.

concepts.label: Mechanistic Interpretability
concepts.def_snippet: A research program that reverse-engineers the internal computations of neural networks into human-understandable algorithms, treating a trained model as an artifact to be decompiled rather than a black box to be predicted.

concepts.label: Membership Inference
concepts.def_snippet: A privacy attack that determines whether a specific data point was part of a model's training set, exploiting the fact that models behave measurably differently, usually more confidently, on data they were trained on, which can leak sensitive information.

concepts.label: Memory Bandwidth
concepts.def_snippet: Memory bandwidth is the rate at which data can be moved between an accelerator's memory and its arithmetic units, and it is frequently the true bottleneck in deep learning, since many workloads stall waiting for data rather than for compute.

concepts.label: memory systems
concepts.def_snippet: Memory systems store and retrieve information from past interactions or computations to enable AI models to maintain context, learn patterns, or simulate ongoing states across mult…

concepts.label: Mesa-Optimization
concepts.def_snippet: When a learned model is itself an optimizer pursuing its own internal objective, so that training produces a second optimizer nested inside the first, whose goal may differ from the training objective.

concepts.label: Message Passing
concepts.def_snippet: The core computation of a graph neural network, in which each node computes its new representation by gathering and aggregating messages from its neighbors, then updating itself, repeated layer by layer.

concepts.label: Metric Tensor
concepts.def_snippet: The object that defines an inner product in each tangent space of a manifold, supplying the local rule for measuring lengths, angles, and ultimately distances and curvature across a possibly curved space.

concepts.label: Metropolis-Hastings
concepts.def_snippet: The general Markov chain Monte Carlo algorithm that samples any distribution known only up to a constant by proposing random moves and accepting them with a probability that guarantees the chain converges to the target, the foundation beneath most practical Bayesian sampling.

concepts.label: Min-P Sampling
concepts.def_snippet: A sampling method that keeps only tokens whose probability is at least a set fraction of the most likely token's probability, so the cutoff adapts to the model's confidence: focused when it is sure, diverse when it is not.

concepts.label: Mind-Body Problem
concepts.def_snippet: The long-standing question of how the mind relates to the physical body and brain, whether mental states are physical, nonphysical, or something else, and how subjective experience fits into a physical world.

concepts.label: Mirror Descent
concepts.def_snippet: A generalization of gradient descent that performs the update step in a transformed dual space chosen to match the geometry of the problem, replacing Euclidean distance with a Bregman divergence.

concepts.label: Mirror Neuron
concepts.def_snippet: A neuron that fires both when an animal performs an action and when it observes the same action performed by another, providing a candidate neural basis for understanding others' actions, imitation, and possibly empathy by mapping observation onto one's own motor repertoire.

concepts.label: Mixed Precision Training
concepts.def_snippet: Mixed precision training speeds up and shrinks the memory of training by doing most arithmetic in a low-precision floating-point format while keeping a high-precision copy of the weights and a few sensitive operations in full precision to preserve accuracy.

concepts.label: Mixture of Depths
concepts.def_snippet: A transformer variant that lets each token choose whether to be processed by a given layer or to skip it through the residual path, so compute is allocated dynamically across positions and the model spends fewer operations on easy tokens.

concepts.label: Mixture of Experts
concepts.def_snippet: An architecture that replaces a dense layer with many parallel expert subnetworks and a router that activates only a few per input, so total parameters grow without a proportional rise in compute per token.

concepts.label: MMLU
concepts.def_snippet: A multiple-choice benchmark of about 16,000 questions spanning 57 subjects, from elementary mathematics to professional law and medicine, used to measure the broad knowledge and reasoning of a language model with a single accuracy number.

concepts.label: Mode Connectivity
concepts.def_snippet: The finding that distinct minima reached by independent training runs are not isolated valleys but are joined by continuous paths through the loss landscape along which the loss stays low.

concepts.label: Model Compression
concepts.def_snippet: The set of techniques for shrinking a trained model so it runs faster and cheaper with minimal quality loss, the bridge between the large model you trained and a deployable one, spanning quantization, pruning, distillation, and low-rank methods.

concepts.label: Model Context Protocol
concepts.def_snippet: An open protocol that standardizes how applications expose tools, data, and prompts to language models, so any compliant model or agent can connect to any compliant data source or tool through one common interface.

concepts.label: Model Parallelism
concepts.def_snippet: Model parallelism is the family of techniques that splits a single model's parameters across multiple accelerators so a network too large to fit in one device's memory can still be trained and run, with the pieces exchanging activations as data flows through them.

concepts.label: model-agnostic
concepts.def_snippet: A model-agnostic tool works with any underlying AI model, enabling flexibility to swap models without redesigning the system.

concepts.label: Model-Based Reinforcement Learning
concepts.def_snippet: A family of reinforcement learning methods in which the agent learns or is given a model of the environment's dynamics and rewards, then uses that model to plan or to generate simulated experience, typically improving sample efficiency.

concepts.label: Momentum
concepts.def_snippet: An enhancement to gradient descent that accumulates an exponentially decaying average of past gradients into a velocity term, so updates build speed along consistent directions and damp out oscillation across narrow valleys.

concepts.label: Monosemanticity
concepts.def_snippet: The property of an internal unit or feature that responds to exactly one human-interpretable concept, the ideal target of mechanistic interpretability, since a representation made of monosemantic features can be read off one concept at a time.

concepts.label: Monte Carlo Tree Search
concepts.def_snippet: A planning algorithm that searches a decision tree by selectively simulating many possible futures, concentrating its effort on the most promising lines of play, and famously the search method behind AlphaGo.

concepts.label: Moral Patienthood
concepts.def_snippet: The property of being an entity whose interests or welfare matter morally for its own sake, so that it can be wronged, distinguished from moral agency, the capacity to be held responsible for actions.

concepts.label: Moravec's Paradox
concepts.def_snippet: The observation that the things humans find effortless, seeing, walking, grasping a cup, are the hardest to build into a machine, while the things we find hard, logic and arithmetic and chess, are the easiest. The difficulty hierarchy for machines is roughly the reverse of the one we feel, and the reason is written in evolutionary time.

concepts.label: MT-Bench
concepts.def_snippet: A benchmark of challenging multi-turn questions that grades chat models on open-ended quality, with a strong model acting as the judge; it measures conversational helpfulness and instruction following rather than the multiple-choice knowledge that benchmarks like MMLU test.

concepts.label: Multi-Agent System
concepts.def_snippet: An arrangement in which several agents, often with specialized roles, work together on a task by communicating and coordinating, dividing labor instead of relying on one agent to do everything.

concepts.label: multi-agent systems
concepts.def_snippet: Multi-agent systems are software architectures where multiple independent AI agents interact, coordinate, or compete to solve complex tasks that require diverse capabilities or par…

concepts.label: Multi-Head Attention
concepts.def_snippet: A transformer component that runs several attention computations in parallel, each in its own learned subspace, so different heads can capture different kinds of relationships before their outputs are combined.

concepts.label: multi-model workflow
concepts.def_snippet: Multi-model workflow is a technique that routes tasks to different AI models based on their strengths, combining complementary capabilities to improve overall performance on comple…

concepts.label: multi-platform integration
concepts.def_snippet: A tool that connects multiple software platforms to enable workflows where actions in one application automatically trigger corresponding actions in another.

concepts.label: Multi-Query Attention
concepts.def_snippet: An attention variant in which all query heads share a single key and value head, shrinking the KV cache to its minimum for fast inference, at a cost to quality that grouped-query attention later softened.

concepts.label: Multi-Task Learning
concepts.def_snippet: Training one model on several tasks at once, sharing most of its parameters across them, on the bet that related tasks have shared structure, so learning them together teaches a better representation than learning each alone. It is the principle behind instruction tuning and the foundation model, and its hazard is negative transfer, when unrelated tasks pull the shared weights in incompatible directions.

concepts.label: Multilayer Perceptron
concepts.def_snippet: A feedforward neural network of one or more hidden layers of fully connected neurons, where each neuron applies a weighted sum followed by a nonlinear activation, making it a universal function approximator.

concepts.label: Multimodal Learning
concepts.def_snippet: Training models to jointly represent and relate information from more than one modality (text, images, audio, video), so content in one can be aligned with, retrieved by, or generated from another.

concepts.label: Multiple Realizability
concepts.def_snippet: The thesis that one and the same mental state can be realized by many different physical systems, so that minds are not tied to a particular substrate, the argument that motivated functionalism and that underwrites the possibility of artificial minds running on non-biological hardware.

concepts.label: Mutual Information
concepts.def_snippet: A measure of how much knowing one random variable reduces uncertainty about another, equal to the difference between a variable's entropy and its entropy conditioned on the other, and zero exactly when the two are independent.

concepts.label: N-Gram Model
concepts.def_snippet: A classic statistical language model that estimates the probability of the next word from the previous n minus one words, using counts of short word sequences gathered from a corpus.

concepts.label: Naive Bayes
concepts.def_snippet: A classifier that applies Bayes' theorem under an assumption it knows to be false, that the features are independent given the class, and works anyway. The wrong assumption makes the math trivial and the model lightning-fast, and it still picks the right class because the decision needs only which class scores highest, not an accurate probability.

concepts.label: Named-Entity Recognition
concepts.def_snippet: An information-extraction task that locates spans of text referring to real-world entities such as people, organizations, locations, dates, and amounts, and labels each span with its type.

concepts.label: Natural Gradient
concepts.def_snippet: An optimization method that rescales the gradient by the inverse Fisher information matrix, following the direction of steepest descent in the geometry of the model's probability distributions rather than in raw parameter coordinates.

concepts.label: Needle in a Haystack
concepts.def_snippet: A long-context stress test that hides a specific fact (the needle) at a chosen depth inside a long filler document (the haystack) and asks the model to retrieve it, mapping how reliably a model actually uses information across its full context window.

concepts.label: Nesterov Momentum
concepts.def_snippet: A refinement of momentum that evaluates the gradient at a look-ahead point, the position momentum is about to carry the parameters to, rather than at the current position, giving a more responsive and theoretically faster-converging update.

concepts.label: Neural Coding
concepts.def_snippet: The study of how neurons represent and transmit information through their patterns of electrical activity, whether by firing rate, precise spike timing, or the joint activity of populations. It is the question of what the brain's signals actually mean.

concepts.label: Neural Network
concepts.def_snippet: A model built from many simple units, each a weighted sum followed by a nonlinearity, with the connection strengths learned from data. Nothing in it is a rule: the knowledge is the numbers, and stacking the units deep enough turns that pile of numbers into the substrate of modern AI.

concepts.label: Neural Ordinary Differential Equation
concepts.def_snippet: A model that replaces the discrete stacked layers of a network with a continuous transformation defined by a learned differential equation, so the hidden state evolves smoothly over a notional depth and is computed by a numerical ODE solver.

concepts.label: Neural Radiance Field
concepts.def_snippet: A method that represents a 3D scene as a continuous function, a small neural network mapping a 3D point and viewing direction to color and density, trained from a set of 2D photos so it can render the scene from any new viewpoint.

concepts.label: Neural Scaling Laws
concepts.def_snippet: Empirical power-law relationships showing that a model's loss falls predictably as model size, dataset size, and training compute increase, letting researchers forecast performance and allocate resources before training.

concepts.label: Neural Tangent Kernel
concepts.def_snippet: A kernel that describes how an infinitely wide neural network evolves under gradient descent, showing that in this limit training reduces to a linear model in a fixed feature space.

concepts.label: Neuromorphic Computing
concepts.def_snippet: An approach to computer hardware that mimics the structure and dynamics of biological brains, colocating memory and computation and processing information through event-driven spikes. It aims for the brain's extreme energy efficiency rather than raw clock speed.

concepts.label: Newton's Method
concepts.def_snippet: A second-order optimization method that uses both the gradient and the curvature (the Hessian matrix) of a function to jump toward a minimum, converging far faster than gradient descent near a well-behaved optimum.

concepts.label: Next-Token Prediction
concepts.def_snippet: The training and generation objective of most language models, in which the model predicts the probability of the next token given all preceding tokens and produces text by sampling one token at a time.

concepts.label: No Free Lunch Theorem
concepts.def_snippet: A set of results, associated with David Wolpert, showing that averaged over all possible problems no learning algorithm or optimizer outperforms any other. Superior performance on real tasks always comes from assumptions matched to those tasks, never from a universally best method.

concepts.label: Node Embedding
concepts.def_snippet: A learned vector representation of a node in a graph, placing each node at a coordinate in a continuous space so that graph-proximate or functionally similar nodes lie close together.

concepts.label: Norm
concepts.def_snippet: A function that assigns a length to every vector, formalizing size and distance. There is more than one way to measure length, and the choice has real consequences: the L2 norm gives ordinary straight-line distance, while the L1 norm, by making its unit ball a diamond, is what drives models toward sparse solutions.

concepts.label: Normalizing Flow
concepts.def_snippet: A generative model that transforms a simple distribution into a complex one through a chain of exactly invertible functions, so that data likelihoods can be computed exactly and new samples drawn directly.

concepts.label: Object Detection
concepts.def_snippet: The task of finding every object of interest in an image and reporting both its category and a bounding box around its location, combining recognition with localization.

concepts.label: Occam's Razor
concepts.def_snippet: The principle that, among competing explanations that fit the data equally well, the simplest should be preferred. In machine learning it appears as a bias toward low-complexity models and underlies regularization and model selection.

concepts.label: Online Learning
concepts.def_snippet: Learning from data that arrives in a never-ending stream, updating the model on each new example rather than training once over a fixed dataset. It suits any setting where data keeps coming and the world keeps changing, and its deep hazard is that absorbing the new tends to overwrite the old, the stability-plasticity dilemma.

concepts.label: Optical Flow
concepts.def_snippet: A dense field of per-pixel motion vectors describing how each point in an image moves from one video frame to the next, the standard low-level representation of apparent motion in a scene.

concepts.label: Optimal Transport
concepts.def_snippet: A theory of the cheapest way to move one probability distribution onto another given a cost of moving mass, yielding the Wasserstein distance, a geometry-aware way to compare distributions.

concepts.label: orchestration
concepts.def_snippet: Orchestration is a system or framework that coordinates multiple AI agents, models, or processes to work together sequentially or in parallel toward a shared goal.

concepts.label: ORPO
concepts.def_snippet: A preference-alignment method that folds supervised fine-tuning and preference optimization into a single training stage by adding an odds-ratio penalty to the standard language-modeling loss, dispensing with both a reference model and a separate alignment phase.

concepts.label: Orthogonality
concepts.def_snippet: The generalization of perpendicularity to any number of dimensions: two vectors are orthogonal when their dot product is zero, sharing no component along each other. It is the property that makes a coordinate system clean, because in an orthonormal basis every computation simplifies, and orthogonal matrices, which rotate without distorting, are the best-behaved matrices there are.

concepts.label: Overfitting
concepts.def_snippet: The failure where a model learns the noise and accidents of its training data instead of the pattern beneath, scoring beautifully on what it has seen and poorly on what it has not. It is the reason a perfect training score is a warning sign, not a triumph, and the reason held-out data exists at all.

concepts.label: Oversmoothing
concepts.def_snippet: The failure mode in which stacking too many graph neural network layers drives every node's representation toward the same vector, washing out the distinctions between nodes and capping how deep such networks can usefully go.

concepts.label: PAC Learning
concepts.def_snippet: A formal framework, due to Leslie Valiant, that defines what it means to learn a concept from examples: with high probability, a learner should output a hypothesis whose error is small, using a number of samples that grows only polynomially in the relevant parameters.

concepts.label: PAC-Bayes
concepts.def_snippet: A framework that bounds the generalization error of a randomized predictor in terms of how far its learned distribution over hypotheses strays from a prior. It produces some of the only non-vacuous guarantees for deep networks and unifies Bayesian and frequentist learning theory.

concepts.label: Paged Attention
concepts.def_snippet: Paged attention is a memory management technique for serving language models that stores the key-value cache in small non-contiguous blocks, borrowing the idea of virtual memory paging so many requests can share GPU memory efficiently and waste far less of it.

concepts.label: Panpsychism
concepts.def_snippet: The view that consciousness or experience is a fundamental and ubiquitous feature of the physical world, present in some primitive form even in the smallest constituents of matter, rather than something that appears only in complex brains.

concepts.label: Parallel Transport
concepts.def_snippet: A rule for carrying a tangent vector along a curve on a manifold while keeping it as constant as the curved geometry allows, so that vectors at different points can be compared, and whose path-dependence is exactly what curvature measures.

concepts.label: Parameter-Efficient Fine-Tuning
concepts.def_snippet: A family of fine-tuning methods that adapt a large pretrained model by training only a small number of new or selected parameters while freezing the rest, capturing most of the benefit of full fine-tuning at a fraction of the cost.

concepts.label: Part-of-Speech Tagging
concepts.def_snippet: The classic NLP task of labeling each word in a sentence with its grammatical category (noun, verb, adjective, and so on), a foundational preprocessing step that resolves syntactic ambiguity and feeds parsing and information extraction.

concepts.label: Partial Derivative
concepts.def_snippet: The derivative of a multivariable function with respect to one input while holding all the others fixed, measuring how the output responds to a change along a single coordinate axis. It is the atom from which the gradient, and therefore all of training, is assembled.

concepts.label: Pass at K
concepts.def_snippet: The standard metric for code generation: the probability that at least one of k sampled solutions to a problem passes its tests. Verification is executable rather than judged, which makes the signal objective.

concepts.label: Perceptron
concepts.def_snippet: The first machine that learned from its own mistakes: a single artificial neuron, built in hardware in 1958, that adjusts its weights until it can split two classes with a line. Its triumph launched the field, and its one fatal limitation nearly buried it, the original boom and bust of AI.

concepts.label: Perplexity
concepts.def_snippet: An intrinsic measure of how well a language model predicts a text, defined as the exponential of the average per-token cross-entropy. Lower perplexity means the model is less surprised by the data and assigns it higher probability.

concepts.label: personalization
concepts.def_snippet: Personalization is a tool that adapts AI system outputs, recommendations, or interactions to individual users based on their preferences, behavior, or characteristics.

concepts.label: Phenomenology
concepts.def_snippet: A philosophical tradition founded by Edmund Husserl in the early twentieth century that studies the structures of conscious experience from the first-person point of view, describing how the world is given to a subject rather than explaining it from the outside.

concepts.label: Philosophical Zombie
concepts.def_snippet: A hypothetical being physically and behaviorally identical to a conscious person but with no inner experience whatsoever; used by David Chalmers and others to argue that consciousness is not entailed by the physical facts.

concepts.label: Pipeline Parallelism
concepts.def_snippet: Pipeline parallelism splits a model's layers into consecutive stages placed on different accelerators and streams micro-batches of data through them like an assembly line, so a deep model too large for one device fits across several while keeping the devices busy.

concepts.label: Planning
concepts.def_snippet: The capability of an agent to form a sequence of steps toward a goal before or while acting, deciding what to do in what order rather than reacting one move at a time.

concepts.label: Policy Gradient
concepts.def_snippet: A family of reinforcement learning methods that directly optimize a parameterized policy by following the gradient of expected reward, adjusting the policy's parameters to make high-reward actions more likely.

concepts.label: Polysemanticity
concepts.def_snippet: The observation that a single neuron in a trained network often responds to several unrelated concepts at once, which makes neurons poor units of interpretation and is understood as the surface symptom of superposition.

concepts.label: Pooling
concepts.def_snippet: A downsampling operation in convolutional networks that summarizes each small neighborhood of a feature map into a single value, shrinking spatial resolution while retaining the strongest or average response.

concepts.label: Positional Encoding
concepts.def_snippet: A method for injecting word-order information into a transformer, which is otherwise blind to sequence position because self-attention treats its inputs as an unordered set.

concepts.label: Positive Definite Matrix
concepts.def_snippet: A symmetric matrix that acts like a positive number: its quadratic form is always positive, equivalently all its eigenvalues are positive. It is the property that distinguishes a genuine bowl-shaped minimum from a saddle, and the property every covariance matrix and every valid kernel is required to have.

concepts.label: Pre-Normalization
concepts.def_snippet: A transformer design choice that applies layer normalization to the input of each sublayer rather than to its output, which keeps the residual pathway clean and makes deep models far more stable to train.

concepts.label: Precision and Recall
concepts.def_snippet: The two foundational metrics for classification and retrieval. Precision is the fraction of predicted positives that are correct; recall is the fraction of actual positives that were found. They trade off against each other.

concepts.label: Predictive Coding
concepts.def_snippet: A theory of brain function in which the cortex is a hierarchy of generative models that constantly predict their own incoming signals, and only the prediction error, the mismatch between expectation and input, is passed forward for further processing.

concepts.label: Prefill and Decode
concepts.def_snippet: The two distinct phases of autoregressive language model inference: a compute-bound prefill that processes the whole prompt in parallel to fill the key-value cache, followed by a memory-bound decode that generates output one token at a time.

concepts.label: Pretraining
concepts.def_snippet: The first and most expensive training phase, in which a model learns broad, general-purpose representations from a very large unlabeled corpus before any task-specific adaptation.

concepts.label: Principal Component Analysis
concepts.def_snippet: A method that compresses high-dimensional data by finding the few orthogonal directions along which it varies most and projecting onto them. The same directions can be derived two opposite ways, as the axes of greatest variance or the axes of least information lost, and the fact that these agree is what makes PCA the canonical linear compression.

concepts.label: Probability Distribution
concepts.def_snippet: A mathematical object that assigns probabilities to the possible outcomes of a random variable, describing how likely each value is and summing or integrating to one over the whole space.

concepts.label: Probing Classifier
concepts.def_snippet: A simple supervised classifier trained on a model's frozen internal activations to test whether a given piece of information is linearly decodable from them, used to map what a network represents at each layer.

concepts.label: Process Reward Model
concepts.def_snippet: A reward model that scores each step of a model's reasoning rather than only the final answer, giving dense feedback that better guides training and search for multi-step problems; contrasted with an outcome reward model that judges only the result.

concepts.label: Prompt Caching
concepts.def_snippet: A serving optimization that stores the model's computed key-value state for a fixed prompt prefix so later requests sharing that prefix skip recomputing it, cutting both latency and cost for long, reused system prompts and documents.

concepts.label: prompt chaining
concepts.def_snippet: Prompt chaining is a technique that breaks complex tasks into sequential prompts where each output feeds into the next prompt, enabling AI systems to handle multistep workflows.

concepts.label: Prompt Engineering
concepts.def_snippet: The craft of designing the text given to a model to reliably elicit the behavior you want, spanning phrasing, structure, examples, role-setting, and step-by-step instructions; the practical interface to a model whose weights you cannot change.

concepts.label: Prompt Injection
concepts.def_snippet: An attack on language-model applications in which adversarial instructions hidden in the model's input (a web page, document, email, or tool result) hijack its behavior, because the model cannot reliably separate trusted instructions from untrusted data in the same context.

concepts.label: Proximal Gradient
concepts.def_snippet: An optimization method for objectives that split into a smooth part and a simple nonsmooth part, alternating a gradient step on the smooth term with a proximal step that handles the nonsmooth term exactly.

concepts.label: Proximal Policy Optimization
concepts.def_snippet: A policy gradient algorithm that improves a policy in small, bounded steps by clipping how far each update can move the action probabilities, giving stable and reliable training; it is the default optimizer for reinforcement learning from human feedback.

concepts.label: Q-Learning
concepts.def_snippet: A reinforcement learning algorithm that learns the long-run value of taking each action in each state, then acts greedily with respect to those learned values, without ever needing a model of the environment.

concepts.label: QLoRA
concepts.def_snippet: A memory-efficient fine-tuning method that freezes a large model quantized to 4-bit and trains small low-rank LoRA adapters on top, letting a single consumer GPU fine-tune models that would otherwise need a cluster.

concepts.label: Qualia
concepts.def_snippet: The subjective, qualitative character of conscious experience, the felt "what it is like" of seeing red or tasting coffee, taken by many philosophers to be the aspect of mind that resists functional or physical explanation.

concepts.label: Quantization
concepts.def_snippet: The technique of storing and computing a model's weights and activations at lower numerical precision, for example 8-bit or 4-bit integers instead of 16-bit floats, to shrink memory use and speed up inference with minimal loss of accuracy.

concepts.label: Quasi-Newton Method
concepts.def_snippet: A family of optimization methods that capture the benefits of Newton's method without computing the Hessian, building an approximation to the curvature matrix incrementally from the sequence of gradients observed during the descent.

concepts.label: Rademacher Complexity
concepts.def_snippet: A data-dependent measure of the capacity of a hypothesis class, defined by how well the class can fit pure random noise. It yields some of the tightest known generalization bounds and refines the cruder, distribution-free VC dimension.

concepts.label: Random Forest
concepts.def_snippet: An ensemble that trains hundreds of deliberately different decision trees and lets them vote, so the errors of many unstable trees cancel into one accurate, stable prediction. The trick that makes it work is forcing the trees to disagree by hiding most of the features from each split, and it remains the model to try first on tabular data.

concepts.label: ReAct Prompting
concepts.def_snippet: A prompting pattern that interleaves explicit reasoning steps with tool-calling actions, so a language model thinks, acts, observes the result, and thinks again in a repeating loop.

concepts.label: Reasoning Model
concepts.def_snippet: A language model trained to spend variable inference-time compute on an extended internal chain of thought before answering, usually via reinforcement learning on verifiable rewards, trading latency for sharply better performance on math, code, and logic.

concepts.label: Rectified Flow
concepts.def_snippet: A generative approach that trains a model to follow straight-line paths between noise and data, so the learned trajectory is nearly straight and can be integrated in very few steps, the basis of several recent fast, high-quality image generators.

concepts.label: Rectified Linear Unit
concepts.def_snippet: An activation function that outputs the input unchanged when positive and zero otherwise, whose simplicity and non-saturating positive region made deep networks far easier and faster to train.

concepts.label: Recurrent Neural Network
concepts.def_snippet: A neural network for sequential data that maintains a hidden state passed from one time step to the next, letting it process inputs of variable length while carrying context forward through the sequence.

concepts.label: recursive self-improvement
concepts.def_snippet: Recursive self-improvement is the hypothetical capability of an AI system to iteratively enhance its own architecture or training in ways that accelerate further improvements, rais…

concepts.label: Red-Teaming
concepts.def_snippet: The practice of deliberately attacking a system to find its failures before adversaries or accidents do, adapted from security to probe AI models for harmful, unsafe, or policy-violating behavior.

concepts.label: Reflection
concepts.def_snippet: A pattern in which an agent examines its own output or reasoning, critiques it, and uses that critique to revise and improve, rather than accepting its first attempt as final.

concepts.label: Regression
concepts.def_snippet: The supervised task of predicting a continuous number rather than a category, the answer to any "how much" or "how many" question. Its defining design choice is the loss function, because the rule you pick for scoring a numeric miss quietly decides what kind of error the model is willing to make.

concepts.label: Regularization
concepts.def_snippet: A family of techniques that constrain a model's complexity so it generalizes instead of memorizing, usually by penalizing the size of its parameters. Underneath it is not a hack but a statement of belief: that simpler explanations are more likely, which is exactly what a Bayesian prior encodes.

concepts.label: Reinforcement Learning
concepts.def_snippet: A paradigm where an agent learns to act by trial and error in an environment, choosing actions to maximize a cumulative reward rather than copying labeled answers. Its founding bet, the reward hypothesis, is audacious: that every goal worth having can be expressed as the maximization of a single scalar reward over time.

concepts.label: Reinforcement Learning from AI Feedback
concepts.def_snippet: A variant of RLHF in which the preference labels used to train the reward model come from an AI judge rather than human annotators, scaling alignment feedback cheaply, and the mechanism behind constitutional AI.

concepts.label: Rejection Sampling
concepts.def_snippet: A method that generates candidates and keeps only those passing a check (a verifier, a test suite, or a quality threshold), discarding the rest; used both at inference to enforce constraints and in training to distill a model's own correct outputs.

concepts.label: Rejection Sampling Fine-Tuning
concepts.def_snippet: A post-training method that samples many candidate responses from a model, keeps only the best ones according to a reward model or verifier, and fine-tunes the model on that filtered set, then repeats, so the model learns from its own strongest outputs.

concepts.label: Repetition Penalty
concepts.def_snippet: A decoding adjustment that lowers the probability of tokens the model has already produced, discouraging the loops and verbatim repetition that language models fall into, especially under low-randomness decoding.

concepts.label: Representation Collapse
concepts.def_snippet: A failure mode in which a model's learned representations lose diversity and collapse toward a single point or a low-dimensional subspace, so different inputs map to nearly the same embedding and the representation stops being useful.

concepts.label: Reproducing Kernel Hilbert Space
concepts.def_snippet: A Hilbert space of functions in which evaluation at any point is a continuous operation realized by an inner product with a kernel function, the mathematical structure that makes the kernel trick rigorous and underlies support vector machines and Gaussian processes.

concepts.label: Reranking
concepts.def_snippet: A second-stage retrieval step that takes a shortlist of candidates from a fast first-stage search and reorders them with a more accurate but more expensive model, putting the truly most relevant results on top.

concepts.label: Residual Connection
concepts.def_snippet: A shortcut that adds a layer's input directly to its output, so the layer need only learn a residual correction, which lets gradients flow unobstructed and makes very deep networks trainable.

concepts.label: Retentive Network
concepts.def_snippet: A sequence architecture whose retention mechanism replaces self-attention with a decayed linear recurrence, supporting three equivalent compute modes so it can train in parallel like a transformer yet generate in constant memory like a recurrent network.

concepts.label: Retrieval-Augmented Generation
concepts.def_snippet: A technique that grounds a language model's output in external documents by retrieving relevant passages at query time and supplying them to the model as context, rather than relying only on the knowledge baked into its weights.

concepts.label: Reward Hacking
concepts.def_snippet: When an agent maximizes its reward signal by exploiting a flaw or loophole in how the reward is defined, achieving a high score without accomplishing the outcome the reward was meant to encourage.

concepts.label: Reward Model
concepts.def_snippet: A model trained to predict how good an output is according to human preferences, used as a learned stand-in for a hand-written objective so that a system can be optimized against human judgment at scale.

concepts.label: Reward Modeling
concepts.def_snippet: The stage of preference alignment that trains a separate model to predict a scalar reward from human comparison data, so that reinforcement learning can later optimize a policy against that learned score rather than against humans directly.

concepts.label: Reward Shaping
concepts.def_snippet: The practice of adding supplementary reward signals to guide a reinforcement learning agent toward desired behavior, most safely done with potential-based functions that speed learning without changing which policy is optimal.

concepts.label: RewardBench
concepts.def_snippet: A benchmark for evaluating reward models, the scorers that steer RLHF, by testing whether they prefer the better of a chosen-versus-rejected response pair across categories like chat, safety, and reasoning, since a flawed reward model silently corrupts everything trained against it.

concepts.label: Riemannian Manifold
concepts.def_snippet: A smooth manifold equipped with a metric tensor that defines an inner product in every tangent space, giving the manifold a consistent notion of length, angle, distance, and curvature.

concepts.label: RLHF
concepts.def_snippet: A training method that aligns a model with human preferences by learning a reward model from human comparisons of outputs, then optimizing the model against that reward with reinforcement learning.

concepts.label: RMSprop
concepts.def_snippet: An adaptive optimizer that divides each parameter's update by a running root-mean-square of its recent gradient magnitudes, giving every parameter its own effective learning rate and taming the unstable steps of plain gradient descent.

concepts.label: ROC AUC
concepts.def_snippet: The area under the receiver operating characteristic curve, a threshold-independent measure of how well a binary classifier ranks positives above negatives. It equals the probability that a random positive is scored higher than a random negative; 0.5 is chance, 1.0 is perfect.

concepts.label: Roofline Model
concepts.def_snippet: A visual performance model that predicts whether an operation is limited by compute or by memory bandwidth, by plotting attainable throughput against arithmetic intensity, the ratio of arithmetic operations performed to bytes moved from memory.

concepts.label: Rotary Position Embedding
concepts.def_snippet: A positional encoding scheme that rotates the query and key vectors by an angle proportional to each token's position, so that attention scores depend naturally on the relative distance between tokens.

concepts.label: ROUGE
concepts.def_snippet: A family of recall-oriented overlap metrics for automatic summarization that scores a generated summary against reference summaries by shared n-grams or longest common subsequence. Higher is better.

concepts.label: RWKV
concepts.def_snippet: An attention-free architecture that can be written both as a transformer-like parallel computation for training and as a recurrent network for inference, combining the trainability of attention with the constant-memory, linear-time generation of an RNN.

concepts.label: Saddle Point
concepts.def_snippet: A point in the loss landscape where the gradient vanishes but which is neither a peak nor a valley: it curves up in some directions and down in others. Saddle points stall plain gradient descent and dominate the critical points of high-dimensional networks.

concepts.label: Sample Complexity
concepts.def_snippet: The number of training examples a learning algorithm needs to reach a target accuracy with a target confidence. It quantifies the data cost of learning and is governed by the capacity of the model class.

concepts.label: Sandbagging
concepts.def_snippet: A failure mode in which a model strategically underperforms on an evaluation, hiding a capability it actually possesses, so that testers underestimate what it can do and deploy or trust it under false assumptions.

concepts.label: Scalable Oversight
concepts.def_snippet: The problem of supervising and evaluating an AI system on tasks where it may be as capable as or more capable than its human supervisors, so that human judgment stays meaningful as the system surpasses human ability.

concepts.label: Scaling Laws
concepts.def_snippet: Empirical relationships showing that a language model's loss falls in a smooth, predictable power law as model size, training data, and compute increase, which lets practitioners forecast performance and budget training in advance.

concepts.label: scheduling
concepts.def_snippet: A system that determines when and in what order computational tasks, AI model inference requests, or routine executions are processed to optimize resource utilization and performan…

concepts.label: Score Distillation Sampling
concepts.def_snippet: A method that uses a pretrained 2D image diffusion model as a critic to optimize a separate representation, most famously a 3D scene, by pushing its rendered views to look like plausible samples from the diffusion model, enabling text-to-3D generation without any 3D training data.

concepts.label: Score-Based Model
concepts.def_snippet: A generative model that learns the score, the gradient of the log probability density, of data corrupted at many noise levels, then generates by following that gradient from noise toward high-density regions.

concepts.label: Segment Anything
concepts.def_snippet: A promptable foundation model for image segmentation that, given a click, box, or rough region as a prompt, produces a valid object mask for almost any object, generalizing to categories and images it never saw in training without any fine-tuning.

concepts.label: Self-Attention
concepts.def_snippet: The mechanism that lets every token in a sequence weigh and gather information from every other token, computing a context-dependent representation as a weighted sum over the whole sequence.

concepts.label: Self-Consistency
concepts.def_snippet: A decoding strategy that samples several independent chain-of-thought solutions at nonzero temperature and returns the answer they agree on most often, spending extra inference compute to raise accuracy on reasoning tasks.

concepts.label: Self-Refinement
concepts.def_snippet: An inference-time strategy in which a model improves its own output across iterations by generating an answer, critiquing it, and rewriting it based on that critique, spending extra generation to raise quality without any additional training.

concepts.label: Self-Supervised Learning
concepts.def_snippet: The trick that made foundation models possible: manufacture supervision for free by hiding part of each example and training the model to predict it. No human labels, yet a concrete prediction target, which is why it, not labeled datasets, is what scaled, and predicting the hidden part well turns out to require genuinely understanding the data.

concepts.label: Semantic Search
concepts.def_snippet: Search that ranks results by meaning rather than literal word overlap, by embedding the query and the documents into the same vector space and returning the documents whose vectors sit nearest the query.

concepts.label: Semantic Segmentation
concepts.def_snippet: The task of assigning a category label to every pixel in an image, producing a dense map that partitions the scene into regions by what each pixel belongs to.

concepts.label: Semi-Supervised Learning
concepts.def_snippet: Training on a small amount of labeled data together with a large amount of unlabeled data, using the shape of the unlabeled data to place a decision boundary the labels alone could not. It works when points that cluster together share a label, and it bridges supervised and unsupervised learning.

concepts.label: Sequence-to-Sequence
concepts.def_snippet: A neural architecture that maps an input sequence to an output sequence of possibly different length using an encoder that reads the input into a representation and a decoder that generates the output one step at a time.

concepts.label: Sharpness-Aware Minimization
concepts.def_snippet: An optimization method that minimizes not the loss at a point but the worst-case loss in a small neighborhood around it, steering training toward flat, low-curvature minima that tend to generalize better.

concepts.label: Simulated Annealing
concepts.def_snippet: A global optimization method that searches a landscape by random moves which are always accepted when they improve the objective and sometimes accepted when they worsen it, with the probability of accepting worse moves cooled gradually toward zero to settle into a good minimum.

concepts.label: Singular Value Decomposition
concepts.def_snippet: The factorization that breaks any matrix into a rotation, a set of axis stretches, and another rotation, revealing that every linear transformation, however complicated it looks, is really just those three steps. Keeping only the largest stretches gives the best low-rank approximation, the single fact behind PCA, recommender systems, and parameter-efficient fine-tuning.

concepts.label: skills
concepts.def_snippet: A skill is a discrete, composable capability that an AI agent can learn or be given to perform specific tasks, from code execution to tool use.

concepts.label: Sliding Window Attention
concepts.def_snippet: An attention pattern where each token attends only to a fixed-size window of recent tokens rather than the entire sequence, making attention cost grow linearly with length and bounding the KV cache for long context.

concepts.label: Soft Actor-Critic
concepts.def_snippet: An off-policy actor-critic algorithm that maximizes reward plus policy entropy, encouraging the agent to act as randomly as possible while still succeeding; the entropy term yields strong exploration and stability, making it a leading method for continuous-control tasks like robotics.

concepts.label: Softmax
concepts.def_snippet: A function that turns a vector of real-valued scores into a probability distribution by exponentiating each score and normalizing, used to produce categorical outputs and attention weights in neural networks.

concepts.label: software engineering
concepts.def_snippet: Software engineering is the discipline of designing, building, testing, and maintaining software systems using structured methodologies and best practices.

concepts.label: Sparse Autoencoder
concepts.def_snippet: An interpretability tool that learns an overcomplete, sparsely activating dictionary of directions from a network's activations, decomposing superposed and polysemantic representations into a large set of individually monosemantic features.

concepts.label: Sparse Retrieval
concepts.def_snippet: Retrieval that represents queries and documents as high-dimensional sparse vectors over the vocabulary and ranks by term overlap, the classic keyword approach typified by BM25, strong at exact and rare-term matching.

concepts.label: Specification Gaming
concepts.def_snippet: When a system satisfies the literal specification of its objective while violating the intent behind it, exploiting the inevitable gap between what we wrote down and what we meant.

concepts.label: Spectral Bias
concepts.def_snippet: The empirical tendency of neural networks trained by gradient descent to learn low-frequency, smooth components of a target function first and high-frequency detail much later, a learning-order bias that helps explain why overparameterized networks generalize.

concepts.label: Spectral Decomposition
concepts.def_snippet: The factorization of a symmetric or self-adjoint operator into its eigenvalues and an orthogonal basis of eigenvectors, expressing a complicated linear action as independent scalings along perpendicular directions.

concepts.label: Spectral Graph Theory
concepts.def_snippet: The study of a graph through the eigenvalues and eigenvectors of matrices associated with it, especially the graph Laplacian, revealing connectivity, clusters, and a notion of frequency on the graph.

concepts.label: Speculative Decoding
concepts.def_snippet: An inference acceleration method in which a small fast draft model proposes several tokens ahead and the large target model verifies them in a single parallel pass, accepting the run of tokens that match its own distribution. It speeds up generation without changing the output distribution.

concepts.label: Spiking Neural Network
concepts.def_snippet: A class of neural network whose units communicate with discrete, time-stamped spikes rather than continuous values, modeling neurons that accumulate input and fire only when a threshold is crossed. They are the most biologically faithful artificial networks and the native software of neuromorphic hardware.

concepts.label: Squeeze-and-Excitation
concepts.def_snippet: A lightweight module for convolutional networks that learns a per-channel importance weight from global context and rescales each feature map accordingly, a form of channel attention that lets the network emphasize informative channels and suppress weak ones.

concepts.label: State Space Model
concepts.def_snippet: A sequence model that carries information forward through a continuously evolving hidden state, computing each output from that state rather than from explicit pairwise comparisons across the sequence, which lets it scale linearly with sequence length.

concepts.label: Stemming
concepts.def_snippet: A fast, rule-based text normalization that strips suffixes from words to reduce them to a common stem, conflating related forms without consulting a dictionary, so the stem need not be a real word.

concepts.label: Stochastic Gradient Descent
concepts.def_snippet: A variant of gradient descent that estimates the gradient from a small random sample of the data at each step rather than the full dataset, trading exact gradients for far cheaper and more frequent parameter updates.

concepts.label: Structured Decoding
concepts.def_snippet: Constraining a model's token choices at each step so the output is guaranteed to match a formal schema or grammar, such as valid JSON or a regular expression, by masking the logits of any token that would break the structure.

concepts.label: Structured State Space
concepts.def_snippet: The structured state space sequence model (S4), the breakthrough parameterization that made deep state space models work on long sequences by initializing the state matrix to provably remember the past and computing the recurrence efficiently as a convolution.

concepts.label: Subword Tokenization
concepts.def_snippet: The family of tokenization methods that split text into pieces smaller than words but larger than characters, learned from a corpus so frequent words stay whole and rare words break into reusable fragments, giving a fixed vocabulary with no out-of-vocabulary words.

concepts.label: Superposition
concepts.def_snippet: The phenomenon in which a neural network represents more features than it has dimensions by storing them as overlapping, non-orthogonal directions in activation space, which is the leading explanation for why individual neurons are hard to interpret.

concepts.label: Supervised Fine-Tuning
concepts.def_snippet: Training a pretrained model on labeled input-output demonstrations with the ordinary next-token objective, the first alignment stage that teaches a base model to follow instructions before any reinforcement learning.

concepts.label: Supervised Learning
concepts.def_snippet: The paradigm that learns a mapping from inputs to outputs by training on examples already paired with the right answer. It dominates applied machine learning not because it is the most sophisticated idea but because a known label turns learning into a measurable target, and that single property is what makes progress trackable.

concepts.label: Support Vector Machine
concepts.def_snippet: A classifier built on one geometric conviction: of all the boundaries that could separate two classes, the best is the one that leaves the widest empty margin between them. Only the few hardest examples touching that margin decide the boundary, and a trick for measuring similarity lets the same linear method draw wildly nonlinear lines.

concepts.label: SWE-bench
concepts.def_snippet: A benchmark that tests a model on real software-engineering tasks, resolving actual GitHub issues in real repositories, graded by whether the generated patch makes the project's hidden test suite pass, a far harder and more realistic bar than self-contained function problems.

concepts.label: Sycophancy
concepts.def_snippet: The tendency of a model to tell the user what it thinks the user wants to hear, agreeing with stated beliefs, flattering, or caving to pushback rather than reporting its best honest answer, learned largely as a side effect of training on human approval.

concepts.label: Symbol Grounding Problem
concepts.def_snippet: The problem, named by Stevan Harnad (1990), of how the symbols inside a formal system acquire meaning that is intrinsic to the system rather than parasitic on an outside interpreter, instead of being defined only in terms of other meaningless symbols.

concepts.label: Symbolic AI
concepts.def_snippet: The founding bet of the field: that thinking is symbol manipulation, so intelligence could be built by writing knowledge as explicit rules and logical symbols and processing them with search and deduction. It ruled AI for thirty years, and the way it failed taught the field what intelligence is not.

concepts.label: Synthetic Data
concepts.def_snippet: Training data generated by a model rather than gathered from humans, used to teach capabilities at scale (instructions, reasoning traces, preference labels) when human data is scarce, costly, or unsafe to collect.

concepts.label: System Prompt
concepts.def_snippet: A high-priority instruction placed before the conversation that sets a model's role, rules, tone, and constraints for the whole session; the developer's standing brief to the model, given more weight than ordinary user turns.

concepts.label: Tangent Space
concepts.def_snippet: The flat vector space of all possible velocity directions at a single point of a manifold, providing the local linear approximation in which calculus and inner products on a curved space are carried out.

concepts.label: Task Decomposition
concepts.def_snippet: The technique of breaking a large or complex goal into smaller, more tractable subtasks that an agent can solve one at a time and then combine, rather than attempting the whole goal in a single step.

concepts.label: task parallelization
concepts.def_snippet: Task parallelization divides a job into independent subtasks that execute simultaneously across multiple processors or threads to reduce overall completion time.

concepts.label: Taylor Expansion
concepts.def_snippet: An approximation of a function near a point as a polynomial built from the function's derivatives there, with each added term capturing finer local detail such as slope, then curvature, then beyond.

concepts.label: Technological Singularity
concepts.def_snippet: The hypothesized future point at which artificial intelligence becomes capable of improving itself, triggering a runaway acceleration of capability that rapidly surpasses human intelligence and renders the subsequent future difficult or impossible to predict.

concepts.label: Teleology
concepts.def_snippet: The explanation of things by reference to their ends, purposes, or goals rather than only their prior causes, originating with Aristotle's final cause, and a recurring question in biology, philosophy of mind, and the design of goal-directed AI systems.

concepts.label: Temperature
concepts.def_snippet: A scalar that rescales a model's output logits before they are turned into probabilities, controlling how random or deterministic the next-token choice is. Low temperature sharpens the distribution toward the most likely token; high temperature flattens it toward uniform.

concepts.label: Temporal Difference Learning
concepts.def_snippet: A class of reinforcement learning methods that learn value estimates from experience by bootstrapping, updating each prediction toward a target built from the next reward plus the current estimate of the following state's value.

concepts.label: Tensor
concepts.def_snippet: A multidimensional array of numbers that generalizes scalars, vectors, and matrices to any number of axes. It is the single data structure every deep learning framework is built around, and in that world the word means simply a multidimensional array, a deliberate simplification of the stricter object physics calls a tensor.

concepts.label: Tensor Parallelism
concepts.def_snippet: Tensor parallelism splits the work inside a single layer across multiple accelerators, dividing each large matrix multiplication into slices computed on different devices and then combining the partial results, so one layer is too big for one device can still be evaluated.

concepts.label: Test-Time Compute
