concepts.def_snippet: The strategy of spending more computation at inference time, rather than only at training time, to improve a model's answers: generating longer reasoning, sampling many candidates, or searching, so accuracy scales with the compute spent per query.

concepts.label: Text-to-Image Generation
concepts.def_snippet: The task and model class that generate an image from a natural-language description, today dominated by diffusion models conditioned on a text embedding, with prompt adherence controlled by classifier-free guidance.

concepts.label: Text-to-Speech
concepts.def_snippet: The task of synthesizing natural-sounding speech from text, the output side of voice interfaces; modern neural systems capture prosody, timbre, and emotion, and can clone a voice from seconds of sample audio.

concepts.label: Text-to-Video Generation
concepts.def_snippet: The task and model class that generate a short video clip from a natural-language description, extending diffusion-based image generation across time so the model must produce frames that are individually realistic and consistent and smoothly moving as a sequence.

concepts.label: TF-IDF
concepts.def_snippet: A classic weighting scheme that scores how important a term is to a document by multiplying how often the term appears in that document by how rare the term is across the whole collection.

concepts.label: The Bitter Lesson
concepts.def_snippet: Rich Sutton's 2019 observation that, across seventy years of AI, general methods that scale with computation have repeatedly beaten methods that build in human knowledge by hand. It is called bitter because the thing it factors out, your own hard-won understanding of the problem, is exactly what you most wanted to matter.

concepts.label: The China Brain
concepts.def_snippet: A thought experiment in which the population of a nation simulates the functional organization of a brain by passing signals, used to challenge functionalism by asking whether such a system would have a unified conscious mind, or any experience at all.

concepts.label: The Knowledge Argument
concepts.def_snippet: Frank Jackson's 1982 thought experiment, in which a scientist who knows every physical fact about color vision yet has never seen color seems to learn something new on first seeing red, used to argue that physical facts leave out the felt qualities of experience.

concepts.label: Token Embedding
concepts.def_snippet: The lookup layer that maps each discrete token id to a dense, learned vector, turning a sequence of symbols into the continuous representations a transformer can actually compute over.

concepts.label: token optimization
concepts.def_snippet: Token optimization is a technique for reducing input or output token consumption in language model interactions through prompt refinement, caching, or selective information inclusi…

concepts.label: Tokenization
concepts.def_snippet: The step that breaks raw text into discrete units called tokens, the atomic symbols a language model actually reads and predicts, before they are mapped to vectors.

concepts.label: Tool Use
concepts.def_snippet: The general capability of a language model to extend itself by invoking external tools (search, code execution, APIs, databases) and folding their results back into its reasoning, rather than relying only on what it learned in training.

concepts.label: Top-k
concepts.def_snippet: A sampling method that keeps only the k most probable next tokens, discards the rest, renormalizes over the survivors, and samples from them. It caps how far into the tail the model is allowed to reach.

concepts.label: Top-p
concepts.def_snippet: A sampling method that restricts the next-token choice to the smallest set of tokens whose probabilities sum to a threshold p, then samples from that set. The candidate pool grows and shrinks with the model's confidence.

concepts.label: Topic Modeling
concepts.def_snippet: An unsupervised technique for discovering the latent themes running through a collection of documents, representing each document as a mixture of topics and each topic as a distribution over words; Latent Dirichlet Allocation is the classic method.

concepts.label: Topological Data Analysis
concepts.def_snippet: A family of methods that extract the shape of data, its connected pieces, loops, and higher-dimensional voids, using tools from topology to find structure that is robust to noise and invariant to bending or stretching.

concepts.label: Topology
concepts.def_snippet: The study of the properties of shapes and spaces that survive continuous deformation such as stretching and bending but not tearing or gluing, formalizing nearness without relying on distance.

concepts.label: TPU
concepts.def_snippet: A tensor processing unit is a custom chip built specifically to accelerate neural network workloads, organizing its arithmetic around a large hardware array that streams data through a grid of multiply-accumulate units to perform matrix multiplication with very high efficiency.

concepts.label: Transfer Learning
concepts.def_snippet: The principle of reusing knowledge a model learned on one task or dataset to improve learning on a different but related task, rather than training every new model from scratch.

concepts.label: Transformer
concepts.def_snippet: A neural architecture that processes a whole sequence at once through self-attention, having deleted the recurrence and convolution everyone assumed a sequence model needed. That removal is the point: with no step-by-step dependency, the entire sequence trains in parallel, which is what unlocked the scale behind every modern large language model.

concepts.label: Tree of Thoughts
concepts.def_snippet: A reasoning method that explores multiple candidate reasoning steps as a branching tree, evaluating and expanding promising branches and backtracking from dead ends, so a model searches over solution paths instead of committing to one linear chain.

concepts.label: Trust Region
concepts.def_snippet: An optimization strategy that builds a local model of the objective and minimizes it only within a bounded region where the model is trusted to be accurate, expanding or shrinking that region based on how well the model predicted the actual improvement.

concepts.label: Turing Test
concepts.def_snippet: A test of machine intelligence proposed by Alan Turing in 1950: a machine passes if a human judge, trading typed messages with a hidden person and a hidden machine, cannot reliably tell them apart. It replaced the unanswerable "can machines think" with a question you can actually run.

concepts.label: Typical Sampling
concepts.def_snippet: A sampling method that keeps only the tokens whose surprisal is close to the distribution's expected surprisal, trimming both the over-probable and the very-improbable tail so generated text matches the information content the model expects, rather than just the highest-probability tokens.

concepts.label: U-Net
concepts.def_snippet: An encoder-decoder convolutional architecture with skip connections that copy fine spatial detail from the contracting path across to the expanding path, producing pixel-accurate dense predictions and now also serving as the standard denoising backbone in diffusion models.

concepts.label: Underfitting
concepts.def_snippet: The opposite failure to overfitting: a model too simple to capture the real pattern, so it does poorly on the training data and on new data alike. It is the easy failure to diagnose, high error everywhere, and the floor you have to clear before overfitting is even worth worrying about.

concepts.label: Universal Approximation Theorem
concepts.def_snippet: A family of results showing that a feedforward neural network with a single hidden layer of sufficient width can approximate any continuous function on a bounded domain to arbitrary accuracy. It establishes that neural networks are expressive in principle, while saying nothing about whether such a network can be learned.

concepts.label: Unsupervised Learning
concepts.def_snippet: Learning from data that carries no labels, where the goal is to uncover the structure already latent in the inputs: the groups, the directions of variation, the distribution that generated them. Its defining difficulty is the flip side of its freedom: with no answer key, there is often no way to say a result is right.

concepts.label: Value Function
concepts.def_snippet: A function that estimates the expected long-run reward an agent will accumulate from a given state, or from a given state-action pair, under a particular policy; it is the core predictive object that most reinforcement learning algorithms learn.

concepts.label: Vanishing Gradient
concepts.def_snippet: A training pathology in deep networks where gradients shrink exponentially as they propagate backward through many layers, so early layers receive almost no learning signal and effectively stop training.

concepts.label: Variance
concepts.def_snippet: A measure of how far a random variable's values spread from its mean, defined as the expected squared deviation from the expectation, with the standard deviation as its square root.

concepts.label: Variational Autoencoder
concepts.def_snippet: A generative model that learns a probabilistic mapping between data and a continuous latent space, training an encoder and decoder jointly so that sampling from the latent prior and decoding produces new data.

concepts.label: Variational Inference
concepts.def_snippet: An approximate Bayesian method that turns intractable posterior inference into optimization by fitting a simple tractable distribution to the true posterior, minimizing the KL divergence between them.

concepts.label: VC Dimension
concepts.def_snippet: A measure of the capacity or expressive power of a class of models, defined as the size of the largest set of points that the class can label in all possible ways. It controls how many samples are needed to learn and how tightly training error predicts true error.

concepts.label: Vector
concepts.def_snippet: An ordered list of numbers that doubles as a point in space and an arrow pointing to it. It is the universal container of machine learning: a house, a word, an image, a model's parameters, all become vectors, which is what lets a computer do geometry on meaning.

concepts.label: Vector Database
concepts.def_snippet: A datastore built to index and query high-dimensional vectors by similarity, returning the stored vectors nearest a query vector quickly, usually via approximate nearest neighbor search plus metadata filtering.

concepts.label: Vector Space
concepts.def_snippet: Any collection of objects you can add together and scale by numbers while obeying a short list of consistency rules. The point of the abstraction is reach: lists of numbers, polynomials, functions, and images all qualify, so one body of theory, dimension, basis, linear maps, applies to all of them at once.

concepts.label: Vector-Quantized Variational Autoencoder
concepts.def_snippet: An autoencoder that compresses an image into a grid of discrete codes drawn from a learned codebook rather than continuous vectors, turning images into sequences of tokens that an autoregressive model or a diffusion model can then generate.

concepts.label: Verifier
concepts.def_snippet: A model or procedure that judges whether a candidate output is correct, used to select among samples, filter training data, and supply the reward in reinforcement learning on verifiable tasks; verifying is often easier and more reliable than generating.

concepts.label: video generation
concepts.def_snippet: A video generation tool synthesizes realistic video sequences from text prompts, images, or other inputs by learning temporal and spatial patterns from training data.

concepts.label: Vision Transformer
concepts.def_snippet: An architecture that applies the transformer directly to images by cutting each image into a grid of patches, embedding the patches as tokens, and processing them with self-attention, dispensing with convolution.

concepts.label: Vision-Language Model
concepts.def_snippet: A model that jointly processes images and text, typically by projecting visual features into a language model's token space so it can describe, answer questions about, and reason over images alongside text.

concepts.label: vision-language models
concepts.def_snippet: Vision-language models are neural networks trained on paired image and text data to perform tasks requiring understanding of both visual content and natural language descriptions.

concepts.label: Watermarking
concepts.def_snippet: Techniques that embed a detectable, often statistical, signal into AI-generated text or images so an output can later be identified as machine-made, aimed at provenance, misuse detection, and telling synthetic content apart from human work.

concepts.label: Weight Decay
concepts.def_snippet: A regularization technique that pulls the model's weights toward zero at every step, either by adding a penalty on their squared magnitude to the loss function or by shrinking them directly, which discourages overfitting and favors simpler solutions.

concepts.label: Weight Initialization
concepts.def_snippet: How a neural network's weights are set before training begins; a poor scheme makes signals vanish or explode as they pass through the layers and stalls learning, while principled schemes like Xavier and He keep activations and gradients well-scaled.

concepts.label: Weisfeiler-Leman Test
concepts.def_snippet: A classical iterative coloring procedure that tests whether two graphs are structurally identical, and the standard yardstick for the expressive power of graph neural networks, since ordinary message passing can distinguish exactly the graphs the test can.

concepts.label: Word Embedding
concepts.def_snippet: A representation of a word as a dense vector of real numbers, learned so that words used in similar contexts land near each other in the vector space, replacing sparse symbolic word identities with continuous geometry.

concepts.label: word2vec
concepts.def_snippet: A family of shallow neural models, introduced at Google in 2013, that learns dense word embeddings by training a network to predict a word from its neighbors or its neighbors from a word.

concepts.label: workflow orchestration
concepts.def_snippet: Workflow orchestration is a tool that automates the coordination and sequencing of multiple tasks or AI agents to complete complex processes end-to-end.

concepts.label: Working Memory
concepts.def_snippet: The brain's limited-capacity system for holding and manipulating a small amount of information over short timescales, the mental workspace that supports reasoning, language, and planning. Its sharp capacity limit shapes much of human cognition.

concepts.label: World Model
concepts.def_snippet: A learned internal model of how an environment evolves, which an agent uses to predict the consequences of actions and to plan or train inside its own imagination rather than only by acting in the real world.

concepts.label: world models
concepts.def_snippet: World models are learned representations of environmental dynamics that enable agents to predict future states and plan actions without real-world interaction.

concepts.label: YAML workflows
concepts.def_snippet: YAML workflows are configuration files that define multi-step processes for AI agents, specifying task sequences, conditions, and dependencies in a human-readable format.

concepts.label: Zero-Shot Learning
concepts.def_snippet: Asking a model to perform a task from an instruction alone, with no examples, relying on the knowledge and skills it absorbed during pretraining and instruction tuning; the default mode for modern instruction-following models.
